summary: A fungal leaf disease of bhindi that causes dark circular lesions with concentric rings, yellowing around spots, and leaf drying under warm humid conditions.
symptoms: Small, dark brown circular spots on leaves
Spots enlarge to form concentric rings
Yellowing around the spots
Severely affected leaves dry up and fall off
causes: Fungal infection
Survival of fungal spores in infected plant debris
Weed hosts acting as reservoirs
treatment: Foliar spray of Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L)
Application of Azoxystrobin or Difenoconazole for severe cases
precautions: Use certified disease-free seeds
Practice crop rotation with non-host crops
Destroy and burn infected crop debris
Maintain adequate plant spacing for aeration
prevention: Monitor lower leaves early for spotting
Reduce long periods of leaf wetness
Keep the field free of infected residue
spread_conditions: Warm and highly humid weather
Frequent rainfall or heavy dew
Wind-blown rain spreading spores
organic_treatment: Trichoderma viride soil application
Neem seed kernel extract (NSKE) 5% spray
Pseudomonas fluorescens foliar spray
yield_impact: Can reduce yield by 15-30% due to loss of photosynthetic area and pod spotting.
notes: Spreads rapidly during monsoon seasons. Early detection on lower leaves is crucial.

severity_levels.low: Scattered small spots on lower or older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots merging with moderate defoliation
severity_levels.high: Extensive blighting, defoliation, and spots on pods

summary: A fungal leaf spot disease of bhindi that causes dark angular or irregular lesions, leaf rolling, and premature leaf drop in humid warm conditions.
symptoms: Sooty black angular spots on the lower surface of leaves
Brown irregular spots with grey centers and red borders
Leaves roll, wilt, and drop prematurely
Severe infection leads to complete defoliation
causes: Fungal infection
Overwintering of the pathogen in crop debris in the soil
treatment: Spray Chlorothalonil or Carbendazim (1 g/L)
Apply Hexaconazole or Propiconazole in advanced stages
precautions: Avoid close planting to ensure good ventilation
Deep summer ploughing to destroy soil-borne debris
Timely weed control
prevention: Maintain airflow through the canopy
Reduce humidity around plants where possible
Clear infected debris from the field
spread_conditions: High humidity above 80%
Temperatures between 25C and 30C
Dense canopy and poor air circulation
organic_treatment: Garlic and green chilli extract spray
Neem oil (3-5 ml/L) mixed with liquid soap
yield_impact: Yield loss of 20-40% due to defoliation affecting pod size and number.
notes: Often confused with nutrient deficiency; the sooty black fungal growth on the underside is a key sign.

severity_levels.low: Few spots isolated on mature leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots covering large leaf areas with early leaf drop
severity_levels.high: Sooty appearance over much of the canopy with severe defoliation

summary: A downy mildew infection in bhindi that begins as pale yellow angular spots and can progress to necrosis, leaf death, and stunted growth when leaf wetness is prolonged.
symptoms: Pale green to yellow angular spots on the upper leaf surface
Purplish-grey or white fluffy growth on the underside of corresponding spots
Leaves turn necrotic and die as spots coalesce
Stunted growth of the plant
causes: Oomycete water mold infection
Prolonged leaf wetness
treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb at 2 g/L
Spray Fosetyl-Al or Copper fungicides
precautions: Water at the base of the plants and avoid overhead watering
Improve soil drainage
Prune and stake plants to improve airflow
prevention: Limit long periods of leaf wetness
Keep spacing open to reduce humidity
Scout upper and lower leaf surfaces early
spread_conditions: Cool nights and warm days with high humidity
Morning dew or overhead irrigation
Overcrowded plant populations
organic_treatment: Bordeaux mixture (1%) spray
Bacillus subtilis based bio-fungicides
yield_impact: Can cause 30-50% yield reduction if it occurs early in the vegetative stage.
notes: Downy mildew pathogens are highly specific, and humidity control is one of the best defenses.

severity_levels.low: Faint yellowing on older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Distinct angular spots with visible underside fuzz
severity_levels.high: Leaves browning, curling, and dying rapidly

summary: A severe viral disease of bhindi that causes curling and puckering of leaves, stunting, and malformed pods, and is spread by whiteflies.
symptoms: Curling, rolling, and puckering of leaves
Thickening of veins and leaf-like enations on the underside of leaves
Severe stunting of the plant
Pods become small, deformed, tough, and pale in color
causes: Viral infection caused by a begomovirus
Transmission by whitefly Bemisia tabaci
treatment: No direct chemical cure for the virus; vector control is required
Spray systemic insecticides like Imidacloprid (0.5 ml/L) or Thiamethoxam (0.3 g/L) to control whiteflies
Spray Spiromesifen to control whitefly nymphs
precautions: Grow virus-resistant or tolerant varieties such as Arka Anamika
Install yellow sticky traps to monitor and catch whiteflies
Use nylon net barriers in nurseries
Uproot and destroy infected plants immediately
prevention: Control whitefly populations early
Remove weed hosts that can carry the virus
Inspect young plants frequently for curling symptoms
scientific_name: Bhendi yellow vein mosaic begomovirus / Enation leaf curl virus
spread_conditions: High populations of whiteflies
Dry and warm weather which favors whitefly breeding
Presence of alternative weed hosts for the virus
organic_treatment: Neem oil (5 ml/L) to deter whitefly feeding
Beauveria bassiana or Verticillium lecanii for biological vector control
yield_impact: Can cause up to 80-100% crop loss if infection occurs during early growth stages.
notes: Vector control is essential because there is no direct cure once plants are infected.

severity_levels.low: Slight curling on new growth
severity_levels.medium: Visible enations and stunted growth
severity_levels.high: Extreme dwarfing, no flowering, and woody twisted pods

summary: A fungal leaf spot disease of bhindi that causes brown lesions with grey centers, dark margins, and sometimes visible pycnidia, leading to tearing and reduced leaf area.
symptoms: Small circular to irregular brown spots
Spots develop an ashy-grey center with a dark brown or reddish margin
Tiny black dots may appear in the center of the spots
Leaves may tear around the lesions creating a shot-hole appearance
causes: Fungal infection
Pathogen survives in seeds and infected plant debris
treatment: Spray Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) or Mancozeb (2.5 g/L)
Apply Carbendazim if spots are spreading rapidly
precautions: Use clean certified seeds
Treat seeds with Thiram or Captan before sowing
Remove and burn fallen infected leaves
Maintain proper field hygiene and weed clearance
prevention: Maintain seed and field sanitation
Watch for grey-centered lesions with dark borders
Reduce spread from splashing irrigation water
spread_conditions: Warm temperatures combined with high rainfall
Overhead irrigation splashing spores onto healthy leaves
Poor field sanitation
organic_treatment: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride (4 g/kg seed)
Panchagavya foliar spray (3%)
yield_impact: Generally a minor disease, but severe infections can cause 10-15% yield loss due to plant stress.
notes: Tiny black dots in the grey centers help distinguish this disease from other leaf spots.

severity_levels.low: Minor spotting with no leaf drop
severity_levels.medium: Increased spotting with shot-holes
severity_levels.high: Severe leaf tearing, blighting, and reduced photosynthetic area

summary: A fungal disease of blackgram that causes dark sunken lesions on leaves, stems, pods, and seeds, and can lead to pod shriveling under humid warm conditions.
symptoms: Dark brown, sunken, circular spots on leaves and pods
Elongated lesions on stems that may girdle the stem
Pinkish spore masses in lesion centers during humid weather
Drying and shriveling of pods under severe infection
causes: Fungal infection
Seed-borne infection and survival in infected plant debris in the soil
treatment: Seed treatment with Carbendazim (2 g/kg) or Thiram (3 g/kg)
Foliar spray of Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) or Carbendazim (1 g/L)
Apply Propiconazole or Hexaconazole for severe outbreaks
precautions: Use disease-free certified seeds
Practice crop rotation with non-leguminous crops
Destroy infected crop debris after harvest
Avoid field operations when foliage is wet
prevention: Use clean seed lots
Reduce carryover of the pathogen in field residues
Limit splash spread during wet conditions
spread_conditions: High humidity and frequent rainfall
Warm temperatures between 25C and 30C
Rain splash and wind spreading spores
organic_treatment: Seed treatment with Trichoderma viride (4 g/kg seed)
Spray of Pseudomonas fluorescens (0.2%)
Neem oil spray (3 ml/L)
yield_impact: Can reduce yield by 20-50% depending on infection stage; pod infection severely affects seed quality.
notes: Using disease-free seeds is one of the most effective ways to prevent primary infection.

severity_levels.low: Small scattered brown spots on lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Lesions spreading to stems and young pods with some defoliation
severity_levels.high: Stem girdling, severe pod spotting, visible pink spore masses, and plant death

summary: A viral disease of blackgram that causes enlarged crinkled leaves, bushy stunted growth, malformed inflorescences, and severe reduction in pod formation.
symptoms: Leaves become enlarged, coarse, and heavily crinkled or puckered
Shortened internodes leading to a stunted bushy appearance
Malformed inflorescence with large leafy bracts
Delayed or absent flowering with very poor pod formation
causes: Viral infection
Highly seed-borne and mechanically transmissible through sap
Transmission by vectors such as whiteflies and aphids
treatment: No chemical cure exists for the virus itself
Control vectors using systemic insecticides such as Dimethoate (2 ml/L) or Imidacloprid (0.5 ml/L) in early stages
precautions: Do not use seeds from infected fields
Rogue infected plants as soon as they are spotted, especially within the first 40 days
Control weeds that act as alternate hosts
prevention: Use seed from clean healthy fields
Control sap-sucking insect populations early
Remove alternate weed hosts
spread_conditions: Sowing infected seeds from previous seasons
High populations of sap-sucking insects
Presence of alternative weed hosts
organic_treatment: Neem Seed Kernel Extract (NSKE) 5% spray to deter vectors
Yellow sticky traps for monitoring and trapping insects
yield_impact: Causes drastic yield losses of 60-100% if plants are infected before the flowering stage.
notes: Symptoms often appear prominently on the third trifoliate leaf. Infected seeds may be slightly larger and heavier than healthy ones.

severity_levels.low: Mild crinkling on newer leaves with normal flowering
severity_levels.medium: Distinct puckering, bushy growth, and reduced pod setting
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, malformed flowers, and no pod formation

summary: A powdery mildew disease of blackgram that starts as white patches on leaves and can spread over leaves, stems, and pods, causing yellowing, defoliation, and forced maturity.
symptoms: White powdery patches initially on the upper leaf surface
Patches coalesce to cover leaves, stems, and pods
Severely infected leaves turn yellow, curl, and drop prematurely
Defoliation can force early crop maturity
causes: Fungal infection by an obligate parasite
Survival on alternate hosts and in plant debris
treatment: Spray Wettable Sulphur (3 g/L) at the first sign of disease
Apply Dinocap (1 ml/L), Hexaconazole (1 ml/L), or Propiconazole (1 ml/L)
Repeat spray after 10-15 days if necessary
precautions: Ensure timely sowing of the crop
Avoid dense planting to allow proper air circulation
Remove and destroy crop debris
prevention: Monitor the crop early in favorable weather
Keep canopy ventilation good
Clean up infected residue after harvest
spread_conditions: Dry weather during the day and cool nights
High morning humidity and dew
Late-sown crops are more susceptible
organic_treatment: Baking soda solution (5 g/L) mixed with mild soap
Panchagavya (3%) foliar spray
Diluted cow milk spray
yield_impact: Can reduce yield by 20-40% due to loss of photosynthetic area and premature defoliation.
notes: It is often a major problem in the Rabi season because cool dry weather favors disease development.

severity_levels.low: Faint white powdery spots scattered on older leaves
severity_levels.medium: White patches covering large leaf areas with slight yellowing
severity_levels.high: Entire plant coated in white powder with severe defoliation and shriveled pods

summary: A highly destructive viral disease of blackgram that causes yellow and green mosaic patches, severe stunting, and poor pod development, and is spread by whiteflies.
symptoms: Irregular alternating yellow and green patches on leaves
In severe cases, leaves turn completely yellow or whitish
Stunted plant growth and delayed maturity
Pods become yellow, stunted, and produce small shriveled seeds
causes: Viral infection caused by Mungbean Yellow Mosaic Virus
Transmission by whitefly Bemisia tabaci
Not seed-borne
treatment: No chemical treatment exists for the virus itself; manage the whitefly vector
Seed treatment with Imidacloprid (5 g/kg seed) for early protection
Foliar spray of Thiamethoxam (0.2 g/L), Acetamiprid (0.2 g/L), or Dimethoate (2 ml/L) to control whiteflies
precautions: Cultivate resistant or tolerant varieties such as VBN 4, VBN 6, T 9, and PU 31
Install yellow sticky traps to manage whiteflies
Uproot and destroy infected plants immediately
Maintain a weed-free field
prevention: Control whitefly populations early
Eliminate alternate weed and legume hosts
Inspect upper leaves frequently for early mosaic symptoms
spread_conditions: High temperatures and dry periods favoring whitefly multiplication
Presence of infected legume crops or weeds nearby
organic_treatment: Neem oil (5 ml/L) or NSKE 5% spray to disrupt whitefly feeding and breeding
Spray Verticillium lecanii for biological control of whiteflies
yield_impact: Early infection can lead to 70-100% yield loss.
notes: This is one of the most destructive diseases of blackgram; resistant varieties and early vector control are the main management tools.

severity_levels.low: Faint yellow speckling on a few upper leaves
severity_levels.medium: Distinct yellow mosaic patterns across the canopy with some stunting
severity_levels.high: Complete yellowing of foliage, severe stunting, and very few small pods

summary: The bhindi plant appears healthy, with no clear visible disease lesions, mildew growth, curling, or shot-hole symptoms. Healthy bhindi performs best in warm conditions with well-drained soil, balanced fertilization, steady moisture, and management that reduces foliage wetness and weed competition.
symptoms: Leaves are uniformly green with normal shape and expansion
New growth is upright and not obviously curled, puckered, or distorted
No obvious concentric leaf spots, grey-centered lesions, fluffy underside growth, or shot-hole tearing are visible
Plant vigor, flowering, and pod development continue normally under balanced water and nutrient supply
causes: Use of treated or disease-free seed planted into warm soil for rapid establishment
Well-drained soil, adequate sunlight, and spacing that supports air circulation
Supplemental irrigation that maintains soil moisture without prolonged foliage wetness
Balanced fertilization based on soil testing and regular field scouting
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue irrigation to maintain soil moisture without waterlogging or long wet leaf periods
Continue soil-test-based fertilization and avoid excessive nitrogen
Keep scouting regularly and remove over-mature or damaged pods during harvest
precautions: Keep weeds controlled early so they do not compete with young plants or harbor pests
Prefer irrigation methods that reduce canopy wetness, such as drip irrigation where practical
Avoid over-applying nitrogen because excessive vegetative growth can delay flowering and fruit set
Rotate away from fields with heavy nematode or wilt pressure when possible
prevention: Plant into warm soil using clean treated seed
Maintain good within-row spacing and field sanitation
Reduce wetness periods in the canopy through irrigation timing and spacing
Inspect the crop regularly so early disease or insect problems are found before they spread
spread_conditions: Warm growing conditions with well-drained soil
Consistent moisture without prolonged canopy wetness
Adequate spacing for airflow
Regular weed management and routine scouting
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive organic crop care if you are following it
Use well-aged compost or approved organic fertility inputs based on soil need
Use mulches only after soil is warm and in ways that do not create excess disease pressure
yield_impact: A healthy crop is more likely to maintain normal pod set, pod quality, and harvest frequency.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official okra production recommendations and the absence of the common bhindi disease signs used by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth

summary: The blackgram crop appears healthy, with no clear visible anthracnose lesions, powdery coating, yellow mosaic, or leaf crinkling. Healthy blackgram is supported by clean seed, good stand establishment, weed-free early growth, balanced nutrients, and moisture management that avoids both stress and waterlogging.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and normally expanded
No obvious yellow mosaic, white powdery fungal growth, sunken anthracnose lesions, or heavy crinkling are visible
Plants maintain a normal stand and branching habit without obvious stress symptoms
Flowering and pod formation can proceed normally when weeds, moisture stress, and vector pressure are controlled
causes: Use of certified or disease-free seed and appropriate seed inoculation or seed treatment
Well-prepared soil and a crop stand maintained at suitable spacing and plant population
Balanced fertilizer use with avoidance of excessive nitrogen
Early weed control and water management that prevents waterlogging and severe drought stress
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue regular scouting for whiteflies, aphids, mildew, leaf spots, and crinkling symptoms
Keep the field weed-free during early growth and maintain balanced nutrition
Avoid waterlogging and respond quickly if new diseased plants appear
precautions: Use certified seed and keep the field clean from infected debris
Keep the crop weed-free during the first 3 to 4 weeks after sowing
Avoid waterlogging and unnecessary crop stress
Rogue and destroy diseased plants promptly if virus or severe disease symptoms appear later
prevention: Maintain timely hoeing or suitable early weed management
Use judicious fertilizer application and avoid excessive nitrogen
Monitor vector insects early, especially where yellow mosaic pressure is known
Maintain field sanitation and remove damaged plant material and crop debris
spread_conditions: Suitable soil preparation and plant stand establishment
Early weed-free crop growth
Balanced nutrient management
Moisture management that avoids waterlogging and severe stress
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive biological or organic support where used
Use approved seed or soil biological inputs such as Rhizobium, Trichoderma, or Pseudomonas only as part of a broader crop management plan
Continue non-chemical monitoring and sanitation practices
yield_impact: A healthy blackgram crop is more likely to maintain normal flowering, pod set, and yield potential.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official blackgram crop production and IPM guidance plus the absence of the main disease signs represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth

summary: The brinjal plant appears healthy, with no clear evidence of borer entry holes, mosaic mottling, leaf spot expansion, little-leaf symptoms, cottony mold, or wilt. Healthy brinjal performs best under warm growing conditions, consistent moisture, balanced fertility, adequate spacing, and watering practices that keep foliage dry.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green, normally expanded, and free from obvious mottling, severe spotting, or inward curling
Terminal shoots remain turgid without dead-heart symptoms or obvious larval boring damage
Flowers and fruits develop normally without frass-plugged holes, strong deformation, or fruit rot
Plants maintain normal vigor when soil moisture, fertility, and weed pressure are managed well
causes: Use of sturdy healthy transplants without leaf spots or visible disease symptoms
Warm growing conditions with well-drained soil and consistent moisture
Balanced fertilization without excessive nitrogen
Adequate plant spacing, mulching, and watering at the base or through drip irrigation
treatment: No disease or pest treatment is indicated at this stage
Maintain consistent soil moisture and avoid letting plants cycle between drought stress and saturation
Continue balanced side-dress fertilization only as needed and avoid excessive nitrogen
Keep scouting regularly for borer damage, wilting, mosaic symptoms, and new leaf spotting
precautions: Avoid overhead irrigation because wet foliage and splashed soil increase disease risk
Maintain adequate plant spacing and airflow to reduce foliar disease and fruit rot pressure
Rotate away from eggplant and other solanaceous crops where soil-borne disease pressure is known
Keep weeds controlled and remove suspicious diseased plants promptly if symptoms appear later
prevention: Start with healthy spot-free transplants
Water deeply and consistently at the base rather than lightly over the foliage
Use mulch and shallow weed control to limit weed competition and soil splash
Maintain crop rotation and good sanitation to reduce carryover of pests and soil-borne diseases
spread_conditions: Warm-season growth with adequate soil moisture
Proper spacing that allows foliage to dry quickly
Watering at the base or through drip irrigation
Steady weed and field sanitation management
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive cultural and organic crop care if you are following it
Use organic mulches after the soil has warmed to conserve moisture and suppress weeds
Continue routine scouting and sanitation instead of unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy brinjal crop is more likely to maintain normal flowering, fruit set, fruit quality, and marketable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official eggplant production recommendations and the absence of the common brinjal disease and borer symptoms represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease or major pest symptoms are detected and the plant shows normal vegetative and reproductive growth

summary: A major brinjal pest problem dominated by shoot and fruit borer infestation, causing dead hearts, fruit boring, fruit rot, and flower loss when larval attack is not controlled early.
symptoms: Drooping and withering of terminal shoots in the vegetative stage
Boreholes on fruits plugged with insect excreta or frass
Rotting of affected fruits due to secondary bacterial or fungal infection
Shedding of flower buds
causes: Infestation by larvae of Leucinodes orbonalis
Continuous cropping of brinjal and other solanaceous crops
treatment: Spray systemic insecticides like Chlorantraniliprole (0.4 ml/L) or Emamectin benzoate (0.4 g/L)
Apply Spinosad or Flubendiamide at the fruit setting stage
precautions: Collect and destroy infected shoots and fruits regularly
Practice crop rotation with non-solanaceous crops
Install pheromone traps to monitor and trap male moths
Avoid using excessive nitrogenous fertilizers
prevention: Scout terminal shoots and young fruits early for boring symptoms
Remove infested shoots and fruits before larvae complete development
Keep field sanitation strong and avoid overlapping infested crops
aliases: Insect Pest Disease (Shoot and Fruit Borer)
spread_conditions: Warm and humid climate
Overlapping crops providing a continuous food source
Lack of field sanitation
organic_treatment: Release of egg parasitoid Trichogramma chilonis
Foliar spray of Neem Seed Kernel Extract (NSKE) 5%
Application of Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) formulations
yield_impact: Can cause up to 70-90% reduction in marketable yield if left unmanaged.
notes: This is one of the most destructive brinjal pests in Asia, and sprays are most effective before larvae enter shoots or fruits.

severity_levels.low: Wilting of a few terminal shoots
severity_levels.medium: Visible boreholes on 10-20% of fruits with moderate shoot damage
severity_levels.high: Severe fruit rotting, large-scale shoot damage, and heavy crop loss

summary: A fungal leaf spot complex in brinjal that causes dark lesions, yellowing, defoliation, and reduced vigor when warm humid conditions favor spread.
symptoms: Small circular to irregular dark brown spots on older leaves
Spots may show a lighter grey center and yellow halo
Concentric rings may appear within darker lesions
Severe infection leads to yellowing, curling, and premature dropping of leaves
causes: Fungal infection
Pathogen survival in infected plant debris and seeds
treatment: Foliar spray of Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) or Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L)
Apply Azoxystrobin or Carbendazim in severe cases
precautions: Use certified disease-free seeds
Maintain proper plant spacing for better aeration
Remove and destroy heavily infected lower leaves
Avoid overhead irrigation
prevention: Scout lower leaves early for first lesions
Reduce long periods of leaf wetness and improve airflow
Remove infected debris after crop completion
spread_conditions: High humidity and frequent rainfall
Warm temperatures between 25C and 30C
Poor air circulation due to dense planting
organic_treatment: Panchagavya spray (3%)
Pseudomonas fluorescens or Trichoderma viride foliar spray
Neem oil (3 ml/L) to help prevent spore germination
yield_impact: Yield loss of 15-30% can occur due to loss of photosynthetic area and premature defoliation.
notes: Early detection and removal of the first spotted leaves can help slow spread significantly.

severity_levels.low: A few isolated spots on the lowest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots covering significant leaf area with slight defoliation
severity_levels.high: Rapid defoliation, spotting on stems and fruits, and reduced vigor

summary: A viral disease of brinjal that produces mottling, puckering, stunting, and fruit deformity, often spreading through aphids or mechanical handling depending on the virus involved.
symptoms: Light and dark green mottling or mosaic patterns on leaves
Leaves become puckered, blistered, and deformed
Stunted overall plant growth
Fruits may be smaller, deformed, or show yellow blotches
causes: Viral infection
Mechanical sap transmission in some mosaic viruses and aphid-vector transmission in others
treatment: Viruses cannot be cured chemically
Spray systemic insecticides like Imidacloprid (0.5 ml/L) or Dimethoate (2 ml/L) to control aphid vectors where appropriate
precautions: Use resistant varieties if available
Sanitize farm tools and wash hands frequently
Uproot and burn infected plants immediately
Control weeds around the field
prevention: Reduce aphid pressure early
Avoid handling healthy plants after infected plants without sanitation
Remove alternate weed hosts and rogue infected plants quickly
scientific_name: Cucumber Mosaic Virus (CMV) / Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV)
spread_conditions: High aphid populations for CMV spread
Handling infected plants and then healthy ones with contaminated hands or tools
Presence of alternative weed hosts
organic_treatment: Spray Neem oil (5 ml/L) to deter aphids
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and trap vectors
Spray garlic and chilli extract to repel insects
yield_impact: Can reduce yield by 20-50% depending on plant age at infection.
notes: Prevention is critical. Tobacco products can carry TMV risk, so field hygiene matters.

severity_levels.low: Mild mottling on new foliage
severity_levels.medium: Distinct mosaic patterns, slight puckering, and reduced fruit size
severity_levels.high: Severe dwarfing, highly deformed leaves, and unmarketable fruits

summary: A phytoplasma disease of brinjal that causes tiny leaves, shortened internodes, witches-broom growth, flower sterility, and near-complete loss of fruiting when infection becomes severe.
symptoms: Extreme reduction in leaf size with leaves becoming very small and narrow
Shortened internodes causing a bushy witches-broom appearance
Leaves turn pale green or yellow
Floral parts become leaf-like and plants become sterile
causes: Phytoplasma infection
Transmission by leafhopper vectors such as Hishimonus phycitis
treatment: No practical chemical cure exists for the phytoplasma
Spray insecticides like Thiamethoxam (0.3 g/L) or Acetamiprid to control leafhoppers
Tetracycline antibiotics may suppress symptoms temporarily but are not practical for routine field use
precautions: Eradicate weed hosts from the field and surrounding areas
Rogue and destroy infected plants strictly
Use disease-tolerant varieties where available
Use nylon netting in nursery beds to reduce early vector feeding
prevention: Monitor and manage leafhopper populations early
Remove alternate weed hosts such as Datura
Destroy infected plants before vector spread increases
spread_conditions: High populations of leafhoppers
Dry and warm weather favorable for vector multiplication
Presence of infected weed hosts
organic_treatment: Neem oil (5 ml/L) spray to repel leafhoppers
Release of predatory spiders or ladybird beetles to help suppress vector populations
yield_impact: This disease can cause up to 100% yield loss in infected plants because fruit formation may stop entirely.
notes: This is one of the most characteristic and economically damaging brinjal diseases, and infected plants should be removed immediately.

severity_levels.low: Slight yellowing and reduction in size of newest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Bushy appearance begins and flower sterility develops
severity_levels.high: Severe dwarfing, tiny leaves, and complete absence of fruits

summary: A cool-wet fungal stem and fruit rot of brinjal that produces water-soaked lesions, white cottony growth, black sclerotia, and collapse of tissues above the infection point.
symptoms: Water-soaked pale lesions on stems and branches
White fluffy cotton-like fungal growth on infected tissues in humid conditions
Hard black sclerotia form inside or on the surface of stems or fruits
Wilting and death of plant parts above the infection site
causes: Fungal infection
Long-term survival of the pathogen in soil as sclerotia
treatment: Spray fungicides like Carbendazim (1 g/L) or Thiophanate-methyl (1 g/L)
Drench the soil base with Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L)
precautions: Deep summer ploughing to bury sclerotia deeply
Use long crop rotation with non-host crops like cereals or corn
Use wider spacing and staking to improve air circulation
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
prevention: Reduce canopy humidity and avoid overhead irrigation where possible
Improve airflow with spacing and staking
Lower soil inoculum through rotation and sanitation
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 15C to 20C with high soil moisture and humidity
Dense canopy limiting airflow
Overhead irrigation
organic_treatment: Soil application of Trichoderma viride or Bacillus subtilis to parasitize sclerotia
Soil solarization during hot summer months before planting
yield_impact: Can cause 30-50% yield loss and rapid plant death in favorable cool wet conditions.
notes: Once a field is heavily infested with sclerotia, long rotations are often needed to lower risk.

severity_levels.low: Small water-soaked spots on lower stems
severity_levels.medium: White fluffy growth is visible and branches begin to wilt
severity_levels.high: Extensive stem rot, black sclerotia, and complete plant collapse

summary: A destructive wilt problem in brinjal, especially bacterial wilt, that causes sudden green wilting, vascular browning, and plant collapse in warm wet soils.
symptoms: Sudden drooping and wilting of leaves, sometimes beginning on one side of the plant
The plant wilts while still remaining green
Browning of vascular tissues inside the stem
White milky bacterial ooze may stream from a cut stem placed in clear water
causes: Bacterial infection
Soil-borne pathogen entering through root injuries
treatment: Chemical control is usually ineffective once the plant is infected
Soil drenching with Copper Oxychloride (3 g/L) plus Streptocycline (150 ppm) may help limit spread to neighboring plants
precautions: Use resistant varieties or grafted plants on resistant rootstocks where available
Ensure excellent field drainage and use raised beds
Practice crop rotation with paddy or cereals for at least 3 years
Control root-knot nematodes that facilitate bacterial entry
prevention: Avoid root injury and waterlogging
Sanitize tools and manage irrigation water carefully

symptoms: Nitrogen deficiency can cause the whole plant to turn pale green while older leaves yellow and drop
Phosphorus deficiency can cause stunting and dark green leaves with purplish tints
Potassium deficiency can scorch and curl older leaf margins
Calcium deficiency can lead to blossom-end rot and deformation of new leaves
Magnesium deficiency can cause interveinal chlorosis on older leaves
causes: Depleted soil nutrients
Improper soil pH that limits nutrient uptake
Inadequate or imbalanced fertilizer application
Water stress from drought or waterlogging that hinders root absorption
treatment: Apply specific fertilizers based on confirmed deficiency
Use foliar sprays of micronutrient mixtures where rapid correction is needed
Apply Calcium Nitrate for blossom-end rot where calcium movement is limiting
Spray Magnesium Sulfate for magnesium deficiency where appropriate
precautions: Conduct regular soil testing before planting
Apply well-rotted farmyard manure to improve soil health
Maintain soil pH in the suitable range for chilli
Ensure consistent watering to prevent calcium transport problems
prevention: Match fertilizer inputs to soil and crop demand
Check soil moisture and pH before applying heavy fertilizer corrections
Maintain good root health and drainage so nutrients remain available
spread_conditions: Not infectious and often appears uniformly or in soil-driven patches
Heavy rainfall can increase nutrient leaching
Prolonged dry spells can limit uptake
organic_treatment: Top-dress with compost or vermicompost for broad nutrient support
Use bone meal for phosphorus support where suitable
Use wood ash or banana peel compost for potassium support where appropriate
Use crushed eggshells or gypsum in soil for calcium support
yield_impact: Yield can drop by 20-60% depending on the nutrient involved and the crop stage at which deficiency occurs.
notes: Before applying large fertilizer corrections, confirm soil moisture and pH because a locked-out nutrient cannot be corrected simply by adding more of it.

severity_levels.low: Slight discoloration or slow growth with little fruit impact
severity_levels.medium: Visible yellowing or scorching with reduced flowering and fruit set
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, flower drop, and blossom-end rot that makes fruits unmarketable

summary: A fungal white-spot disease of chilli that causes small pale-centered lesions with dark margins, yellowing, and defoliation when humidity and canopy wetness remain high.
symptoms: Small circular sunken spots on leaves that appear white or ash-grey
Spots usually have a dark brown or reddish-brown border
As lesions age, the white center may crack and fall out
Severe infections cause yellowing and leaf drop

Survival on infected solanaceous crop debris and weeds
treatment: Foliar application of broad-spectrum fungicides like Mancozeb (2.5 g/L) or Chlorothalonil
Apply systemic fungicides like Difenoconazole or Azoxystrobin for stronger control where needed
precautions: Remove and destroy crop debris after the season ends
Practice rotation with non-host crops and avoid continuous tomato, potato, eggplant, or chilli cycles
Improve air circulation through spacing and pruning where appropriate
Water plants at the base to avoid wetting the foliage
prevention: Reduce long periods of leaf wetness
Keep the canopy from becoming overcrowded
Scout lower foliage early for first white-centered lesions
spread_conditions: Moderate to warm temperatures
High humidity and extended periods of leaf wetness
Overcrowded plant canopy
organic_treatment: Preventive copper-based sprays such as Copper Oxychloride
Trichoderma viride or Bacillus subtilis bio-fungicide sprays
Neem oil (3 ml/L) application
yield_impact: This is often a minor disease, but severe early defoliation can still reduce yield by 10-25%.
notes: It is often confused with Cercospora leaf spot, but Stemphylium-associated lesions are generally smaller, paler, and less ringed.

severity_levels.low: Few scattered white spots on lower foliage
severity_levels.medium: Numerous spots with some yellowing and leaf fall
severity_levels.high: Heavy defoliation with loss of vigor and greater fruit sunscald risk

summary: The cotton crop appears healthy, with no clear visible bacterial blight lesions, curl-virus distortion, herbicide injury, hopper burn, or widespread reddening. Healthy cotton depends on steady growth, balanced fertility, good weed management, timely irrigation during reproductive growth, and avoiding stresses that reduce canopy function or fruit retention.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and normally expanded without strong angular blight, severe curling, strap-like distortion, or major reddening
Plants maintain normal square, bloom, and boll development with a functional leaf canopy
Shoots and stems remain vigorous without obvious blackarm lesions, severe twisting, or widespread pest burn
The crop continues reproductive growth when moisture, fertility, and pest pressure remain balanced
causes: Use of healthy delinted seed and good stand establishment
Balanced fertility management based on field potential and soil condition
Timely irrigation and stress reduction during key reproductive stages
Good weed control, crop monitoring, and avoidance of off-target herbicide exposure
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Maintain irrigation and field monitoring through squaring, bloom, and boll development
Keep fertility balanced and avoid excessive vegetative growth from over-application of nitrogen
Continue monitoring for whiteflies, jassids, herbicide drift, and leaf spots so problems are caught early
precautions: Avoid prolonged plant stress from drought, waterlogging, compaction, or severe weed competition
Prevent herbicide drift and sprayer contamination near cotton
Keep weed hosts controlled because they can harbor pests and interfere with crop growth
Protect canopy health so carbohydrate production and boll retention stay strong
prevention: Use clean seed and establish a healthy uniform stand
Plan irrigation and fertility around crop stage and growth demand
Maintain effective weed management and timely scouting
Avoid management mistakes that reduce canopy size or fruit retention
spread_conditions: Healthy growth is supported by balanced water and fertility management
Reproductive-stage irrigation is especially important
Low weed pressure and low drift risk improve crop stability
Good canopy health supports carbohydrate supply for fruiting
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care and field sanitation
Use approved organic soil-building practices and residue management where they fit the production system
Rely on monitoring, irrigation timing, and balanced nutrition rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy cotton crop is more likely to maintain canopy function, boll retention, lint yield, and fiber quality.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official cotton growth, irrigation, and fertility recommendations and the absence of the main disease, pest, and injury signs represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease, major pest injury, or physiological disorder symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative and reproductive growth

summary: A bacterial disease of cotton that causes angular leaf spots, vein blackening, blackarm stem lesions, and boll spotting under warm wet conditions.
symptoms: Angular water-soaked spots on leaves
Vein blight leading to blackening of leaf veins
Blackarm symptoms with dark necrotic lesions on stems
Sunken water-soaked blackish spots on bolls

Seed-borne inoculum in infected seed
Survival in infected crop debris
treatment: Copper-based bactericides such as Copper oxychloride
Streptomycin sulphate sprays where appropriate
precautions: Use certified disease-free acid-delinted seed
Grow resistant varieties
Deep ploughing to bury crop residue
Rotate with non-host crops
prevention: Reduce splash spread from rain and irrigation
Start with clean seed lots and resistant material where available
Scout early for angular leaf spotting and vein involvement
spread_conditions: High humidity and heavy rainfall
Warm temperatures around 30C to 35C
Wind-driven rain
Overhead irrigation
organic_treatment: Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens
Neem-based formulations
yield_impact: Yield loss is often 10-30%, but can approach 50% in highly susceptible varieties because of boll damage and rot.

severity_levels.low: Small angular spots restricted to a few lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spread to stems causing blackarm and multiple leaves
severity_levels.high: Severe boll rot, widespread defoliation, and stem girdling

summary: A severe cotton leaf curl virus problem that causes leaf curling, thickened veins, enations, stunting, and major yield loss when whitefly pressure is high.
symptoms: Upward or downward curling of leaf margins
Thickening and swelling of leaf veins
Cup-shaped enations form on the underside of leaves
Plants become stunted
causes: Viral infection
Transmission by whitefly Bemisia tabaci
treatment: No direct chemical cure exists for the virus
Control vectors using systemic insecticides such as Imidacloprid, Thiamethoxam, or Diafenthiuron
precautions: Plant CLCuV-resistant or tolerant cotton varieties
Eradicate alternate weed hosts such as okra and Abutilon
Use synchronized planting of the crop
prevention: Manage whiteflies early and continuously
Reduce alternate host weeds around the field
Rogue heavily affected plants when practical in early stages
spread_conditions: High whitefly populations
Presence of alternate host weeds
Warm dry weather favoring whitefly breeding
organic_treatment: Yellow sticky traps for whiteflies
Neem oil spray to deter whiteflies
Release of beneficial insects such as lacewings
yield_impact: Yield loss can range from 30% to 80% depending on growth stage at infection.
notes: Once the crop is planted, vector control is the main practical management tool because the virus itself cannot be cured chemically.

severity_levels.low: Slight vein thickening on newer leaves
severity_levels.medium: Visible curling, stunting, and enation formation
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, no boll formation, and plant sterility

summary: A chemical injury problem in cotton caused by herbicide drift, contamination, or carryover, resulting in distorted leaves, twisted growth, and malformed reproductive structures.
symptoms: Strapping with narrowing and elongation of leaves
Twisted or epinastic stem and petiole growth
Chlorosis or necrosis depending on the chemical involved
Malformed flowers and squares
causes: Drift from neighboring fields, commonly 2,4-D or Dicamba
Improper tank mixing or contaminated spray equipment
Residual herbicide in the soil
treatment: The injury cannot be cured chemically
Provide optimal irrigation and nutrition so the plant can recover and outgrow damage where possible
precautions: Use drift-reducing nozzles and spray only in calm weather
Thoroughly clean spray tanks before entering cotton fields
Use tolerant traits only with matching approved chemistries where relevant
prevention: Prevent off-target movement during application
Avoid sprayer contamination and carryover
Monitor nearby applications during sensitive cotton stages
spread_conditions: Windy conditions during nearby herbicide application
Temperature inversions trapping vapors
Improper sprayer cleanout
organic_treatment: Application of biostimulants or seaweed extracts to reduce stress
yield_impact: Yield loss is highly variable and can range from minor to severe depending on dose, herbicide type, and growth stage.
notes: Cotton is highly sensitive to phenoxy herbicides such as 2,4-D, so application stewardship around cotton fields is critical.

severity_levels.low: Mild crinkling of leaves with recovery of normal growth
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable leaf strapping and delayed squaring
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, square drop, and death of the growing point

summary: A leaf discoloration problem in cotton that may be genetic, viral, or nutritional, causing irregular yellow, white, and green patches and sometimes reduced vigor.
symptoms: Irregular patches of yellow, white, and green on leaves
No strong necrosis or rotting in early stages
Growth may become stunted if a viral mosaic is involved
causes: Genetic mutation or chimera
Mosaic virus infection in some cases
Severe micronutrient deficiency such as zinc or iron
treatment: Foliar spray of micronutrients such as zinc or iron if deficiency is confirmed
Vector control if a viral cause is suspected
precautions: Conduct soil tests prior to planting
Remove severely variegated plants if viral infection is suspected
prevention: Differentiate harmless genetic variegation from viral or nutrient problems before acting
Maintain balanced micronutrient supply and general crop health
spread_conditions: Vector activity if the cause is viral
Poor soil conditions if the cause is nutritional
organic_treatment: Application of compost tea and balanced organic fertilizers
yield_impact: Yield impact is generally low unless the underlying cause is a severe viral infection or strong systemic nutrient deficiency.
notes: Some variegation is harmless, but field diagnosis should rule out viral mosaic and micronutrient problems before ignoring it.

severity_levels.low: A few leaves show variegation with normal plant growth
severity_levels.medium: Significant discoloration with slight stunting
severity_levels.high: Loss of photosynthetic area leading to severe stunting and low yield

summary: A sap-sucking jassid infestation in cotton that causes downward cupping, yellow to reddish leaf margins, hopper burn, and reduced vigor.
symptoms: Downward cupping of leaves
Yellowing of leaf margins that turn reddish or brownish
Stunted plant growth
Small active wedge-shaped greenish insects are present under leaves
causes: Sap-sucking pest infestation
Injection of toxic saliva into plant tissues
treatment: Systemic insecticides such as Flonicamid, Dinotefuran, or Imidacloprid
Avoid early-season synthetic pyrethroids that may trigger resurgence
precautions: Grow jassid-tolerant or hairy-leaved varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizer
Maintain proper spacing for aeration
prevention: Monitor fields early in the season for jassid populations
Protect natural enemies and avoid flare-ups from unbalanced spray programs
Keep the crop from becoming excessively succulent due to overfertilization
spread_conditions: High humidity with cloudy weather
Excessive nitrogen application
Lack of natural predators
organic_treatment: Neem seed kernel extract (NSKE 5%) or Neem oil
Spraying Beauveria bassiana or Verticillium lecanii
Encouraging ladybird beetles and Chrysoperla
yield_impact: Yield loss can reach 10-25% because photosynthetic activity is reduced and defoliation may increase.
notes: This is often an early-season pest, and its injury can resemble nutrient deficiency or physiological reddening if insects are not checked carefully.

severity_levels.low: Slight yellowing at leaf margins
severity_levels.medium: Pronounced cupping and reddening of leaves
severity_levels.high: Severe hopper burn with drying and defoliation

summary: A physiological reddening disorder of cotton in which leaves turn red or purplish, especially during boll development, often because nutrient supply and plant demand fall out of balance.
symptoms: Leaves turn red or purplish starting from the margins
Veins may remain green initially
Premature leaf aging and drop occur
It is most common during boll maturation
causes: Nitrogen or magnesium deficiency
Sudden drops in nighttime temperature
Waterlogging or moisture stress affecting root uptake
High nutrient demand by developing bolls
treatment: Foliar spray of 2% Urea or 1% Potassium Nitrate
Foliar spray of Magnesium Sulphate at 1% during boll development
precautions: Use balanced basal fertilizer application including N, P, K, and Mg
Improve drainage to prevent waterlogging
Avoid water stress during peak flowering and boll formation
prevention: Maintain soil health and organic matter
Protect the crop from abrupt moisture stress
Monitor nutrient balance as boll load increases
spread_conditions: Poor soil health with low organic matter
Extreme weather fluctuations
Heavy boll load draining nutrients from leaves
organic_treatment: Epsom salt application for magnesium support
Heavy application of farmyard manure or vermicompost before planting
yield_impact: This disorder can reduce yield by 15-20% and may also lower lint quality if it appears early during boll development.
notes: It should be distinguished from hopper burn: physiological reddening often keeps the leaf flatter, while jassid injury more often produces downward cupping.

severity_levels.low: Slight reddening on older lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Reddening spreads into the middle canopy with some defoliation
severity_levels.high: Whole plant appears red with widespread premature defoliation and poorly developed bolls

summary: The cowpea crop appears healthy, with no clear evidence of vascular wilt, mosaic distortion, or Septoria spotting. Healthy cowpea growth is supported by clean seed, warm-season establishment, steady moisture, weed control, and balanced fertility that avoids overuse of nitrogen while supporting nodulation and pod filling.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and normally expanded without obvious mosaic mottling, dark spot complexes, or strong wilting
Plants maintain normal branching, flowering, and pod set without sudden collapse or strong vascular browning
Canopy vigor remains good when weeds are controlled and soil moisture is kept reasonably steady
Healthy stands usually show productive growth with low visible stress and good pod development
causes: Use of high-quality clean seed and good stand establishment
Warm-season growth with appropriate inoculation and moderate fertility
Adequate soil moisture with good drainage
Good rotation, sanitation, and vector management
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Maintain regular scouting for aphids, wilting, and lower-canopy spotting
Avoid excessive nitrogen because cowpea performs well as a legume when nodulation is supported
Keep the field reasonably weed-free and protect roots from stress
precautions: Use certified disease-free seed and avoid infested fields where possible
Maintain drainage to reduce prolonged wet soil conditions
Limit carryover of residues and volunteer hosts that may harbor pathogens or vectors
Monitor aphids and other insect vectors early
prevention: Use crop rotation and field sanitation
Support nodulation with the correct inoculum where needed
Maintain moisture without prolonged waterlogging
Remove diseased plants quickly if wilt or mosaic symptoms appear later
spread_conditions: Warm-season growth with adequate but not excessive moisture
Good nodulation and balanced fertility
Low weed pressure and low vector pressure
Reasonable field sanitation and drainage
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care and sanitation
Use organic soil-building practices and inoculation support where appropriate
Rely on monitoring, drainage, and vector suppression rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy cowpea crop is more likely to maintain normal pod set, seed fill, and marketable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official southern pea and cowpea production recommendations and the absence of the key disease signs represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative and reproductive growth

summary: A severe vascular wilt problem of cowpea that causes sudden leaf flaccidity, internal browning, stem injury, and plant collapse under warm wet conditions.
symptoms: Sudden drooping and flaccidity of leaves without initial yellowing
Vascular browning when the stem is cut open
Stem cankers and cracking in later stages
Complete wilting and collapse of the plant
causes: Bacterial infection invading the vascular system
Infected seeds
Pathogen survival in soil and crop debris
treatment: No highly effective chemical cure exists once plants are infected
Preventive copper-based bactericides may help limit spread
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
precautions: Use certified disease-free seeds
Implement a 2-3 year crop rotation with non-legume crops
Ensure proper field drainage to prevent waterlogging
Sterilize farming tools regularly
prevention: Avoid prolonged wet soil and contaminated irrigation movement
Start with clean seed lots and rotate away from affected fields
Rogue affected plants promptly
scientific_name: Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. vignicola / Ralstonia solanacearum
spread_conditions: High soil moisture and waterlogging
Warm temperatures between 25C and 35C
Spread through irrigation water and contaminated tools
organic_treatment: Soil treatment with beneficial microbes like Pseudomonas fluorescens
Application of neem cake during land preparation
Soil solarization before planting
yield_impact: This disease can cause 30% to 100% yield loss and may destroy entire fields under favorable conditions.
notes: It is often confused with fungal root rots; a milky bacterial ooze from a cut stem in water is a useful field clue.

severity_levels.low: Wilting observed in just one or two branches
severity_levels.medium: Multiple branches wilted with noticeable stunting
severity_levels.high: Rapid complete collapse and death of the plant

summary: A viral mosaic disease of cowpea that causes mottling, leaf distortion, stunting, and reduced pod formation, often spread by insect vectors and infected seed.
symptoms: Mottling and mosaic patterns of light and dark green on leaves
Leaf puckering, blistering, and distortion
Stunted overall plant growth
Reduced pod number and possible pod distortion
causes: Viral infection
Transmission primarily by aphids or beetles
Use of infected seeds
treatment: The virus cannot be chemically cured
Control insect vectors using systemic insecticides such as Imidacloprid or Thiamethoxam
precautions: Plant virus-resistant cowpea varieties where available
Eradicate leguminous weeds around the field
Use early planting to avoid peak aphid populations
Use certified virus-free seed
prevention: Suppress vectors early
Reduce alternate weed hosts nearby
Start with clean seed and rogue infected plants where practical
scientific_name: Cowpea Aphid-Borne Mosaic Virus (CABMV) / Cowpea Mosaic Virus (CPMV)
spread_conditions: High populations of aphid or beetle vectors
Presence of alternative weed hosts nearby
Warm dry weather favoring vector multiplication
organic_treatment: Spray Neem oil or insecticidal soap to suppress aphids
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor vectors
Release natural predators like ladybird beetles
yield_impact: Yield loss can range from 20% to 100%, depending on the crop stage at infection.
notes: Because there is no cure for the virus, management relies on vector control, clean seed, and field sanitation.

severity_levels.low: Mild mottling on younger leaves with normal growth
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable distortion and slight stunting
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, no pod formation, and complete loss of productivity

summary: A fungal leaf spot disease of cowpea that starts as dark reddish or brown lesions and can progress to pycnidia-bearing spots, yellowing, defoliation, and poor pod filling under wet conditions.
symptoms: Small circular to irregular dark brown or reddish spots on leaves

Yellowing around lesions and premature defoliation occur as disease advances

Overwintering of the fungus in infected crop debris
treatment: Apply protective or systemic fungicides such as Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, or Azoxystrobin
Start spraying at the first sign of disease, especially during wet weather
precautions: Deep plowing to bury crop residues after harvest
Maintain wider plant spacing to improve airflow
Use crop rotation with non-host crops for at least 2 years

Scout the lower canopy first because disease often begins there
Remove heavily infected lower leaves where practical

Rain splash spreading spores to lower leaves
Overcrowded planting causing poor air circulation
organic_treatment: Copper-based fungicides approved for organic use
Foliar sprays of biological agents like Trichoderma viride
Regular removal of heavily infected lower leaves

severity_levels.low: A few isolated spots on the lowest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots spread into the middle canopy with some yellowing
severity_levels.high: Heavy spotting, severe premature defoliation, and spread to pods

summary: The groundnut crop appears healthy, with an even green canopy, normal leaf expansion, good vigor, and no clear evidence of early leaf spot, late leaf spot, rust, or broad nutritional stress. Healthy groundnut growth is supported by clean seed, balanced soil fertility, good nodulation, adequate calcium in the pegging zone, steady moisture without waterlogging, and regular scouting through canopy development.
symptoms: Leaves remain evenly green and normally expanded without strong spotting, rust pustules, or widespread chlorosis
Plants maintain normal vigor, branching, flowering, pegging, and pod development for the crop stage
The canopy stays reasonably uniform without rapid defoliation, burn-out patches, or premature drying
Healthy stands often show good root and nodule development when soil pH, calcium, and moisture are favorable
causes: Use of quality seed with good stand establishment
Balanced fertility and soil pH that support nodulation and nutrient uptake
Adequate calcium availability in the pegging zone and consistent moisture during pod development
Good drainage, crop rotation, sanitation, and timely scouting
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue routine scouting for early leaf spot, late leaf spot, rust, and nutrient stress as the canopy closes
Maintain irrigation or moisture management to avoid drought stress and flooding
Support pegging and pod fill with soil-test-based fertility and calcium management where recommended
precautions: Avoid prolonged waterlogging because flooding can weaken nodulation and crop health
Do not allow dense weed growth or heavy residue pressure to increase disease and harvest stress
Maintain field sanitation and reduce volunteer groundnut plants that can carry inoculum
Use balanced nutrition and avoid ignoring calcium needs in the pegging zone
prevention: Use crop rotation and clean planting material
Scout nodulation and early canopy health after establishment
Maintain soil-test-based fertility, including calcium management where needed
Reduce prolonged leaf wetness and respond early if foliar disease symptoms appear
spread_conditions: Balanced fertility, suitable soil pH, and effective nodulation
Adequate but not excessive soil moisture with good drainage
Low disease and weed pressure during canopy development
Supportive calcium availability in the pegging zone
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; maintain preventive crop care, scouting, and field sanitation
Use compost or other soil-building practices according to soil test guidance
Rely on moisture management, rotation, and canopy monitoring rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy groundnut crop is more likely to maintain normal pegging, pod fill, kernel development, and marketable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official peanut and groundnut production recommendations and the absence of the key disease signs represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth, pegging, and pod development

summary: A fungal leaf spot disease of groundnut that appears early in the season as reddish-brown lesions with a distinct yellow halo and can reduce photosynthetic area and crop vigor if it spreads.
symptoms: Sub-circular dark brown to reddish-brown spots on the upper leaf surface
Spots are typically surrounded by a prominent yellow halo
Lesions on the lower leaf surface are lighter brown
Disease commonly appears early in the season, often 3 to 4 weeks after sowing
causes: Fungal infection
Pathogen survival in infected plant debris in the soil
Carryover on volunteer groundnut plants
treatment: Spray fungicides such as Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Tebuconazole
Begin spraying at first appearance of symptoms and repeat after 14 to 15 days if weather remains favorable
precautions: Rotate with non-leguminous crops such as maize or sorghum
Use deep plowing to bury crop debris
Maintain plant spacing that improves canopy airflow
Grow tolerant or resistant varieties where available
prevention: Reduce inoculum from residues and volunteer plants
Scout the lower canopy early because disease begins early in the season
Respond before lesions spread across the canopy
spread_conditions: High relative humidity above 80 percent
Prolonged leaf wetness for 10 to 14 hours
Moderate temperatures around 20C to 24C
organic_treatment: Spray Neem Seed Kernel Extract at 5 percent
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens or Bacillus subtilis formulations
yield_impact: This disease can cause about 15% to 50% yield loss if not controlled because photosynthetic area is lost.
notes: It is often confused with late leaf spot, but the bright yellow halo and earlier onset are key distinctions.

severity_levels.low: A few small spots on the lower older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots enlarge and spread into the mid-canopy with slight yellowing
severity_levels.high: Severe spotting causes premature leaf drop and strong stunting

summary: An unusually early rust problem of groundnut in which orange to reddish-brown pustules develop during early vegetative growth and can greatly reduce crop vigor before pegging if not managed.

Pustules primarily form on the lower leaf surface
Upper leaf surface shows yellowish flecks corresponding to pustules below
Leaves dry and desiccate rapidly, but often remain attached to the plant
causes: Fungal infection
Wind-borne urediniospores from infected neighboring fields
Carryover from volunteer plants
treatment: Apply systemic fungicides such as Hexaconazole, Propiconazole, or Tebuconazole
Sulfur dusting may be used early in the morning where appropriate
precautions: Control weeds and eradicate volunteer groundnut plants
Avoid staggered or contiguous plantings that maintain inoculum
Use early-maturing or rust-resistant varieties where possible
prevention: Scout lower leaf surfaces early if rust has appeared unusually soon in the season
Reduce volunteer hosts and regional carryover
Protect the crop before widespread canopy infection
spread_conditions: High humidity and continuous cloudy weather early in the season
Heavy dew formation
Temperatures around 20C to 25C
organic_treatment: Spray botanical extracts such as Neem oil
Use foliar applications of Trichoderma viride
yield_impact: Early onset rust can cause about 50% to 70% yield loss because it weakens the crop before pegging and pod development.
notes: This differs from the usual rust pattern mainly by its unusually early appearance, which amplifies its damage potential.

severity_levels.low: Few scattered pustules on the lowest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Pustules cover lower leaves and cause browning
severity_levels.high: Rapid spread across the canopy with severe drying and halted pod development

summary: A destructive late-season fungal leaf spot of groundnut that produces dark carbonaceous lesions, rapid defoliation, and severe yield loss when humidity remains high late in the crop cycle.
symptoms: Dark brown to nearly black circular spots, mostly on the lower leaf surface
Yellow halo is usually absent or very faint
Spots may look rough or carbonaceous because of spore masses
Disease appears later in the season, commonly around 50 to 60 days after sowing
Severe infection causes rapid defoliation
causes: Fungal infection
Survival in crop debris and volunteer plants
treatment: Use fungicides such as Tebuconazole, Chlorothalonil, or Carbendazim plus Mancozeb
Time sprays around canopy closure and early symptom onset
precautions: Remove and destroy infected crop debris after harvest
Rotate with cereals or other non-host crops
Plant resistant or tolerant varieties where available
prevention: Scout late-season foliage closely because late leaf spot often escalates rapidly
Lower humidity pressure where possible through crop management
Manage rust and late leaf spot together when they appear as a disease complex

Temperatures around 25C to 30C
Dense canopy trapping moisture
organic_treatment: Apply aqueous leaf extracts of Datura or Neem
Spray biocontrol agents such as Pseudomonas fluorescens
yield_impact: This disease can cause about 50% to 70% yield loss and also reduces fodder quality through severe defoliation.

severity_levels.low: Scattered dark spots on the bottom-most leaves
severity_levels.medium: Coalescing spots with lower leaves beginning to drop
severity_levels.high: Severe defoliation leaves stems bare and lesions spread onto stems and pegs

summary: A non-infectious nutritional disorder in groundnut caused by depleted or unavailable nutrients, often expressed as chlorosis, stunting, poor pegging, or a high proportion of poorly filled pods depending on which nutrient is limiting.
symptoms: Iron deficiency causes interveinal chlorosis on young upper leaves
Calcium deficiency causes poor peg development, darkened plumule, and empty pods or pops
Nitrogen deficiency causes general yellowing of older leaves and weak growth
Zinc deficiency can produce clustered rosette-like leaves and shortened growth
