High soil pH restricting iron or zinc availability
Insufficient moisture that limits nutrient uptake
Lack of calcium in the pegging zone
treatment: Use soil-test-based correction for the deficient nutrient
For iron deficiency, spray Ferrous Sulfate plus Citric acid
For zinc deficiency, apply Zinc Sulfate in soil or as foliar spray
For nitrogen stress, use an appropriate starter dose or foliar urea where justified
precautions: Conduct soil testing before planting
Apply gypsum at pegging stage where calcium is needed
Ensure proper Rhizobium inoculation to support nitrogen fixation
prevention: Maintain balanced fertility and good soil moisture
Monitor soil pH and calcium availability in the pegging zone
Do not assume all yellowing is infectious disease without checking nutrition
spread_conditions: Alkaline or highly weathered soils
Drought stress
Poor nodulation for nitrogen fixation
organic_treatment: Incorporate well-rotted farmyard manure or compost
Use bone meal or crushed eggshells as calcium-supporting amendments where appropriate
Apply liquid seaweed extract as a supportive measure
yield_impact: Yield loss is variable and may range from about 10% to 60%, with calcium deficiency being especially harmful to kernel yield.
notes: Nutrient problems are often mistaken for viral or fungal disease; the pattern of yellowing on new versus old leaves is an important clue.

severity_levels.low: Mild discoloration or slight stunting with little pod impact
severity_levels.medium: Pronounced yellowing and noticeable delay in growth and flowering
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, tissue necrosis, and many empty pods or pops

summary: A foliar rust disease of groundnut that produces numerous reddish-brown pustules, progressive canopy burn, and major yield loss under humid conditions if fungicide protection is delayed.
symptoms: Numerous small elevated reddish-brown to orange pustules on the lower leaf surface
The epidermis over pustules ruptures and exposes a powdery mass of spores
Heavily infected leaves turn brown, wither, and dry up but often remain attached
Pustules may also appear on stems and pegs in severe infections
causes: Fungal infection
Spores dispersed by wind and rain splash
treatment: Apply systemic fungicides such as Tridemorph, Propiconazole, or Hexaconazole
Use Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil as protectants before infection becomes severe
precautions: Use rust-resistant varieties where available
Destroy volunteer groundnut plants and crop debris
Maintain an uncropped break between consecutive groundnut crops in the same area
prevention: Scout the lower leaf surface as canopy closes because rust symptoms often appear later than leaf spot
Shorten spray intervals if rust becomes established
Combine cultural control with fungicide programs to reduce heavy outbreaks
spread_conditions: High relative humidity, heavy dew, and frequent rains
Moderate temperatures around 20C to 25C
Overlapping groundnut cropping seasons in a region
organic_treatment: Foliar application of Neem oil or Neem Seed Kernel Extract
Use Bacillus subtilis sprays as a preventive measure
yield_impact: Groundnut rust can reduce yield by more than 50% and can also ruin fodder quality because of heavy fungal growth on the foliage.
notes: Unlike many leaf spots, rust-infected dead leaves often remain attached rather than dropping early.

severity_levels.low: A few pustules appear in the lower canopy
severity_levels.medium: Pustules spread upward and the foliage starts to look burned or rusty
severity_levels.high: Complete drying of foliage with severe peg stunting and small shriveled kernels

summary: The maize crop appears healthy, with a uniform stand, vigorous green foliage, normal whorl development, and no clear evidence of lethal necrosis, rust pustules, major blight lesions, streaking, or destructive whorl-feeding damage. Healthy maize performance is supported by good seed and spacing, balanced nutrient management, proper irrigation and drainage, weed suppression, and early field scouting to protect crop vigor through vegetative and reproductive growth.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and well expanded without pronounced chlorotic streaking, rust pustules, large blight lesions, or rectangular grey leaf spots
Plants maintain normal stand, height, whorl growth, and canopy development for the stage of the crop
Whorls and leaves do not show major frass buildup, ragged feeding, or dead-heart symptoms
Healthy crops typically sustain good root support, tassel and ear development, and low visible stress when water and nutrition are balanced
causes: Use of healthy seed and good establishment practices
Proper spacing, weed control, and soil-health management
Balanced fertilization based on soil condition without excessive nitrogen
Adequate irrigation and drainage with regular crop monitoring
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue scouting after about 20 days from sowing for early pest, disease, and nutrient issues
Maintain irrigation according to crop need and avoid prolonged moisture stress or waterlogging
Keep the field clean and preserve a healthy stand through balanced nutrient and weed management
precautions: Avoid excessive nitrogen because overly succulent plants can become more vulnerable to pests and diseases
Maintain proper spacing and a healthy crop stand
Use clean seed and supportive soil-health practices including organic manures or biofertilizers where appropriate
Respond early to visible whorl damage, leaf streaking, or foliar spotting
prevention: Select tolerant or resistant varieties where relevant
Use healthy seed and recommended seed treatment or bioprotective measures
Follow crop rotation, proper irrigation, and soil-test-based nutrition
Monitor field conditions, pests, diseases, and nutrient symptoms routinely
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is favored by good stand establishment, balanced nutrition, and proper irrigation
Low weed pressure, good spacing, and field sanitation support canopy health
Early scouting helps detect stress before it expands across the crop
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care and regular scouting
Use soil-health-supporting practices such as compost, green manuring, or biofertilizers where suitable
Rely on crop monitoring, irrigation management, and field sanitation instead of unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy maize crop is more likely to maintain normal canopy function, pollination, ear fill, and grain or fodder yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official maize production and IPM recommendations and the absence of the key model-target disease and pest symptoms.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease or major pest symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth and stand health

summary: A severe viral disease complex of maize caused by co-infection with maize chlorotic mottle virus and a potyvirus, leading to chlorosis, necrosis, dead-heart symptoms, and major ear failure.
symptoms: Severe yellowing and mottling of leaves starting from the base
Necrosis of leaf margins progressing toward the midrib
Dead-heart symptom where the central whorl dies
Premature aging, stunting, and poor cob development
Malformed, small, or partially filled cobs
causes: Simultaneous infection by two viruses such as MCMV and SCMV or MDMV
Transmission by insect vectors including thrips, aphids, and rootworms
Possible introduction through infected seed lots
treatment: There is no chemical cure for viral infection
Control insect vectors early using systemic insecticides where recommended
Rogue and destroy infected plants promptly
precautions: Rotate with non-cereal crops for at least two seasons
Use certified virus-free seed
Plant resistant or tolerant maize varieties where available
Maintain a maize-free break to reduce inoculum and vectors
prevention: Reduce vector populations and alternate hosts around the field
Avoid continuous maize cropping
Start with clean seed and remove infected plants early
scientific_name: Co-infection of Maize Chlorotic Mottle Virus (MCMV) and Potyviridae (e.g., Sugarcane Mosaic Virus - SCMV)
spread_conditions: High vector populations
Continuous maize cropping in the same field
Presence of alternate hosts such as Johnson grass or sorghum
organic_treatment: Neem-based sprays may help deter insect vectors
Intercropping strategies such as push-pull with Desmodium may help reduce vector pressure
yield_impact: This disease can cause about 50% to 100% yield loss and is especially devastating before flowering.
notes: MLN is a synergistic viral disease complex, so field sanitation and vector management are critical.

severity_levels.low: Mild chlorotic mottle on a few leaves with slight stunting
severity_levels.medium: Pronounced yellowing and browning of margins with reduced ear size
severity_levels.high: Complete plant necrosis, dead heart, and death before flowering

summary: A fungal rust disease of maize that causes reddish-brown pustules on leaves and can reduce photosynthetic area and yield, especially under humid conditions.
symptoms: Small circular to elongated reddish-brown pustules on both leaf surfaces
Pustules rupture and release powdery rust-colored spores
Late-season pustules may darken to black
Heavily infected leaves yellow and die prematurely
causes: Fungal infection
Wind-blown spores from infected fields or alternate hosts
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides containing Pyraclostrobin, Azoxystrobin, or Propiconazole
Treat when pustules are visible on lower leaves before tasseling for best protection
precautions: Plant rust-resistant hybrids
Use early planting to avoid peak late-season spore loads
Manage volunteer corn and alternate hosts
prevention: Scout both leaf surfaces regularly as canopy develops
Protect the crop before upper-canopy spread becomes severe
Maintain crop health to reduce secondary stalk-rot risk
scientific_name: Puccinia sorghi (Common Rust) / Puccinia polysora (Southern Rust)
spread_conditions: Cool to warm humid conditions depending on rust type
Frequent heavy dew or light rains
Wind movement of spores
organic_treatment: Use wettable sulfur or copper-based fungicides where appropriate
Apply biocontrol agents such as Bacillus subtilis
yield_impact: Yield loss is often around 10% to 30%, but severe southern rust can cause much greater damage.
notes: Southern rust is usually more destructive than common rust because it destroys leaf tissue more rapidly in warm weather.

severity_levels.low: A few scattered pustules on lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Pustules spread into the middle canopy with minor yellowing
severity_levels.high: Heavy pustule density on most leaves with severe tissue death and stalk-rot vulnerability

summary: A destructive maize pest caused by Spodoptera frugiperda larvae that feed in the whorl and on developing ears, producing window-paning, ragged leaves, heavy frass, and severe yield loss when infestations build early.
symptoms: Window-paning on young leaves
Ragged torn holes in leaves as larvae grow
Large amounts of sawdust-like frass in the whorl
Deep feeding in the whorl that can destroy the growing point
Larvae may burrow into the side of the ear
causes: Infestation by the larval stage of fall armyworm moths
Egg masses laid on maize leaves by adult moths
treatment: Apply insecticides such as Emamectin benzoate, Spinetoram, or Chlorantraniliprole directly into the whorl
Rotate insecticide classes to slow resistance development
precautions: Plant early and synchronize sowing where possible
Use Bt maize where permitted and appropriate
Deep plow to expose pupae
Scout fields beginning about one week after germination
prevention: Break the continuous food supply created by staggered plantings
Detect whorl infestations early before larvae become large
Preserve field hygiene and regular scouting
spread_conditions: Warm humid climates that allow continuous breeding
Wind-assisted movement of adult moths
Staggered maize planting that provides continuous food
organic_treatment: Apply Neem-based formulations with soap as an adjuvant
Use push-pull systems with Desmodium and Napier grass
Place sand or ash-lime mixtures into infested whorls where locally practiced
Use biopesticides such as Beauveria bassiana or NPV
yield_impact: This pest can cause about 20% to 70% yield loss depending on growth stage and intensity of whorl or ear damage.
notes: The inverted Y mark on the larval head and four dark rear spots are classic identification features.

severity_levels.low: Minor window-paning with less than 5% of plants infested
severity_levels.medium: Visible ragged leaves and frass in whorls with about 10% to 20% infestation
severity_levels.high: Widespread whorl destruction, ear damage, and heavy defoliation

summary: A chewing-pest problem in maize caused by grasshoppers that feed on leaves, silks, and tassels, sometimes causing severe border damage and pollination loss during outbreak years.
symptoms: Irregular chewing on leaf margins
Severe defoliation may leave only midribs
Silks can be chewed and pollination may be disrupted
Grasshoppers are often visible jumping or flying in the field
causes: Feeding by adult and nymph grasshoppers
Migration from drying weeds, grassland, or field borders
treatment: Apply insecticides such as Lambda-cyhalothrin, Deltamethrin, or Malathion to field margins before migration intensifies
Treat nymph stages early for best results
precautions: Control weeds along borders to reduce breeding sites
Use tillage in the appropriate season to destroy egg pods
prevention: Monitor field edges because infestations often start from borders
Manage nearby weeds and grassy refuge areas
Respond before silk clipping and heavy defoliation become widespread
spread_conditions: Hot dry weather following a wet spring
Dense weeds along borders serving as hatching sites
organic_treatment: Use Nosema locustae where appropriate
Spray garlic or hot pepper extract as a deterrent
Encourage natural predators such as birds and spiders
yield_impact: Yield loss is often low to moderate, but severe outbreaks or heavy silk clipping can drive much larger losses.
notes: Border treatments are often effective because invasion usually starts from field edges.

severity_levels.low: Minor chewing on outer leaves with negligible impact
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable leaf damage moving deeper into the field
severity_levels.high: Complete defoliation or silk clipping that leads to barren ears

summary: A maize leaf-feeding beetle problem that is most damaging to seedlings and young plants, causing shot holes, scraping, and possible stand suppression when feeding is heavy.
symptoms: Small round holes chewed through leaves
Upper leaf surface scraping that leaves white elongated streaks
Damage is most noticeable on seedlings and young plants
Small metallic or dark beetles may be seen jumping or moving on leaves
causes: Feeding by adult beetles and larvae
Overwintering adults emerging in spring to attack young maize
treatment: Apply foliar pyrethroids or neonicotinoids when feeding crosses economic thresholds
Use seed treatments such as Thiamethoxam or Imidacloprid as strong preventive protection where appropriate
precautions: Use insecticidal seed treatment to protect vulnerable seedlings
Promote rapid seedling growth with proper planting depth and fertilization
Keep fields and borders weed-free
prevention: Reduce alternate grass hosts and weedy borders
Protect seedlings during the most vulnerable stage
Scout early emerging crops closely
scientific_name: Various (e.g., Oulema melanopus, Chaetocnema pulicaria / Flea beetles)
spread_conditions: Cool prolonged springs that slow maize growth
Weedy fields containing alternate grass hosts
organic_treatment: Use diatomaceous earth around young plants
Apply kaolin clay as a physical barrier
Use Neem oil as a suppressive option
yield_impact: Direct feeding often causes less than 5% loss, but vectoring of bacterial disease can increase economic risk.
notes: Some leaf beetles are especially important because they can vector bacterial pathogens such as Stewart's wilt.

severity_levels.low: A few small holes or streaks on seedling leaves
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable shot holes or scraping with slight growth slowdown
severity_levels.high: Severe leaf skeletonization, seedling stunting, or death

summary: A major fungal blight of maize caused by Exserohilum turcicum that produces large cigar-shaped lesions and can sharply reduce yield if it reaches the upper canopy before or around silking.
symptoms: Large elongated cigar-shaped greyish-green to tan lesions on leaves
Lesions may range from about 1 to 6 inches long
Dark grey spores can form in lesion centers under humid conditions
Lesions may merge and kill entire leaves
Disease often begins on lower leaves and moves upward
causes: Fungal infection
Survival of the pathogen in infected maize debris on the soil surface
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides such as Mancozeb, Azoxystrobin, or Propiconazole
Treat between tasseling and silking if lesions approach the ear leaf
precautions: Plant resistant hybrids
Use tillage to bury residue and speed decomposition
Rotate away from maize for 1 to 2 years
prevention: Scout the lower canopy early and protect the ear leaf zone
Reduce carryover inoculum from infected residue
Respond before lesions coalesce across the canopy
spread_conditions: Moderate temperatures around 18C to 27C
Prolonged heavy dew, overcast conditions, and high humidity
Continuous maize cultivation
organic_treatment: Use copper-based fungicides where appropriate
Apply compost teas or Bacillus subtilis formulations as suppressive aids
yield_impact: Yield loss can reach about 30% to 50% when disease establishes before or during silking.
notes: The large cigar-shaped lesion is the key feature that separates northern corn leaf blight from grey leaf spot.

severity_levels.low: A few lesions on the lowest leaves before silking
severity_levels.medium: Lesions spread to the middle canopy and reduce photosynthetic area
severity_levels.high: Lesions merge, blight leaves above the ear, and cause rapid decline

summary: A fungal grey leaf spot disease of maize that starts as tan flecks and progresses to rectangular vein-limited lesions, leading to canopy blight and major yield loss if it reaches the ear leaf and upper canopy.
symptoms: Small tan spots with yellow halos on lower leaves
Lesions elongate into rectangular vein-restricted spots
Mature lesions turn greyish and blocky and may reach 2 to 3 inches
Severe cases cause browning and blighting of entire leaves as lesions coalesce
causes: Fungal infection
Survival of the pathogen in undecomposed maize residue
treatment: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicides at VT or R1 when thresholds are met
Spray is often justified once lesions are present on the third leaf below the ear
precautions: Use highly resistant hybrids
Bury infected residues where feasible
Rotate with non-host crops such as soybeans or alfalfa
prevention: Scout lower leaves and the ear leaf zone as humidity rises
Reduce long-term residue carryover in fields with repeated maize
Protect the upper canopy before heavy blighting develops
spread_conditions: Warm to hot weather around 25C to 30C
Very high humidity above 90 percent and prolonged leaf wetness
No-till or minimum-tillage systems with residue carryover
organic_treatment: Use strict crop rotation and residue management as the main organic strategy
Preventive copper sprays may help in limited settings
yield_impact: This is one of the most yield-limiting maize foliar diseases and can cause about 20% to 50% loss through canopy destruction and secondary stalk rots.
notes: The sharp rectangular lesions restricted by veins are the clearest diagnostic feature.

severity_levels.low: Scattered rectangular lesions on bottom leaves only
severity_levels.medium: Lesions reach the ear leaf with noticeable greying of the lower canopy
severity_levels.high: Complete blighting of the upper canopy with strong stalk-rot vulnerability

summary: A severe viral disease of maize spread by leafhoppers that causes vein-parallel streaking, stunting, and frequent ear failure when infection occurs early in the crop.
symptoms: Narrow yellow or white broken streaks run parallel to the leaf veins
Streaking appears first on the youngest leaves
Plants become severely stunted with shortened internodes
Plants may produce tiny deformed cobs or no cobs at all

Transmission exclusively by leafhoppers of the genus Cicadulina
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the virus itself
Seed treatment with systemic insecticides can help protect seedlings from leafhoppers early
Foliar insecticides may be used where leafhopper pressure is high
precautions: Plant resistant maize varieties where available
Use early planting to avoid peak leafhopper populations
Maintain a weed-free barrier around the field
Remove symptomatic plants early when practical
prevention: Reduce alternate grass hosts around the crop
Protect seedlings during the first weeks after emergence
Avoid late planting that increases vector exposure
spread_conditions: Erratic rainfall followed by dry spells that promote leafhopper movement
Late planting
Presence of wild grass alternate hosts
organic_treatment: Reflective mulches may deter leafhoppers in smaller-scale settings
Apply Neem-based botanical insecticides early against vectors
Intercrop with non-host crops to disrupt vector movement
yield_impact: Infection within the first 3 to 4 weeks after emergence can lead to complete crop failure.
notes: The virus is not spread mechanically or by seed; it depends on the leafhopper vector.

severity_levels.low: Faint sparse streaking on new leaves with minimal stunting
severity_levels.medium: Dense yellow streaking across new leaves with moderate stunting
severity_levels.high: Severely stunted plants with intense streaking and barren stalks

summary: The onion crop appears healthy, with upright green foliage, normal neck and bulb development, and no clear evidence of blights, mildew, rust, bacterial collapse, or severe thrips-linked stress. Healthy onion performance is supported by friable well-drained soil, balanced fertility without excessive late nitrogen, careful irrigation that avoids prolonged leaf wetness, weed control, and good curing conditions as bulbs mature.

Plants maintain normal neck and bulb development for the growth stage without strong softening, dieback, or tip collapse
Bulbs and necks continue to size and mature without obvious rotting, foul odor, or heavy distortion
Healthy stands usually show even growth when moisture and fertility are managed carefully and stress is minimized

Balanced fertility and irrigation management
Good soil tilth, drainage, and weed suppression
Careful curing and handling as bulbs mature

Continue scouting for thrips, foliar blights, mildew, and bulb rots as the crop progresses
Maintain irrigation to support growth while minimizing prolonged leaf wetness
Avoid heavy late nitrogen that encourages thick necks and poor curing
precautions: Keep the field and cull areas clean to reduce inoculum and pest carryover

Promote strong curing and storage hygiene

Reduced stress and good curing practices improve bulb quality and storage performance
Low weed pressure and good airflow support canopy health

Use compost or other soil-improving inputs according to crop need
Rely on monitoring, irrigation management, and clean curing practices instead of unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy onion crop is more likely to maintain bulb sizing, neck maturity, storage quality, and marketable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official onion production recommendations and the absence of the key disease and pest symptoms represented by the model.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal leaf health, neck development, and bulb progress

summary: An opportunistic Alternaria leaf blight of onion that often colonizes damaged or stressed foliage and causes dark concentric lesions, tip dieback, and reduced leaf area.

Survival of the pathogen in plant debris
treatment: Use foliar fungicides such as Mancozeb, Chlorothalonil, or Azoxystrobin
Apply at early symptom onset
precautions: Maintain field hygiene and remove crop debris
Control primary pests such as thrips and diseases such as downy mildew
Provide spacing and airflow that reduce prolonged leaf wetness

Scout older leaves and damaged tissue early
Limit canopy humidity and residue carryover

Warm temperatures around 20C to 25C
Plant stress or prior pest damage

Apply Neem oil to help reduce thrips injury
Use Bacillus subtilis biological sprays

summary: A cool-weather fungal leaf blight of onion characterized by pale flecking, silvery halos, progressive tip dieback, and canopy collapse when leaf wetness remains prolonged.

Severe spotting causes tips to brown and die back
Heavy infection can collapse much of the leaf canopy

Sclerotia survive in soil, cull piles, or crop residues
treatment: Use protectant fungicides such as Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb
Use systemic fungicides when disease pressure is high

Avoid excess nitrogen that creates dense canopies
Destroy cull piles and volunteer onions
prevention: Reduce extended leaf wetness and canopy density
Protect the crop during cool wet periods
Keep inoculum sources away from production areas
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 15C to 20C
Long periods of leaf wetness
Poor air circulation
organic_treatment: Use timely copper fungicide applications
Apply biological products such as Bacillus subtilis

Contaminated irrigation water and wet conditions

Let tops dry down and necks seal before harvest
Avoid wounding bulbs during harvest and handling
Store bulbs in cool dry well-ventilated conditions
prevention: Minimize late-season moisture and handling injury
Cure bulbs well before storage
Remove rotting bulbs quickly to prevent spread in storage
spread_conditions: Heavy rainfall or overhead irrigation late in the season
Warm temperatures above 25C
Mechanical damage during harvest or handling
organic_treatment: Focus on proper curing and drying
Use strict sanitation and remove rotting bulbs immediately

severity_levels.low: Softness in a few outer scales with mild leaf yellowing
severity_levels.medium: Internal rot, foul odor, and wilting leaves
severity_levels.high: Complete bulb liquefaction and heavy storage loss

Bulbs become spongy and shriveled

Undercut roots to speed field drying
Store only onions with completely dry tight necks
Maintain low storage temperature and suitable humidity

Avoid bruising and excess neck succulence
Reduce moisture around harvest and storage entry

Thick succulent necks often linked to excess nitrogen
Inadequate curing before storage

Avoid bruising bulbs

severity_levels.medium: Grey mold visible at the neck with upper scale browning

Larvae hatch and begin feeding immediately

Use deep plowing to expose overwintering pupae
Monitor moth flights with pheromone traps

Treat early because older larvae are harder to suppress

Weedy fields that provide alternate hosts

Apply Neem Seed Kernel Extract at 5 percent
Release natural enemies such as Trichogramma wasps

severity_levels.low: Minor feeding on outer leaves

Rotate chemistry to reduce resistance risk

Use a 3 to 4 year crop rotation
Destroy volunteer onions and cull piles
prevention: Reduce morning leaf wetness and dense planting
Scout early in cool humid periods
Avoid overhead watering when disease risk is high
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 10C to 22C
High humidity, heavy dew, or fog
Dense planting
organic_treatment: Use copper-based fungicides
Increase spacing and avoid overhead watering

severity_levels.high: Complete defoliation and halted bulb development

summary: A soil-borne Fusarium basal rot of onion that begins at the basal plate and roots, leading to yellowing, root loss, basal decay, and major field or storage losses.

Roots turn dark brown, rot, and disappear

causes: Soil-borne fungal pathogen
Infection through root wounds or direct invasion of the basal plate
treatment: Use fungicidal seed treatments or root dips such as Carbendazim or Thiram where appropriate
Chemical control is difficult once the disease is established
precautions: Use resistant onion varieties where available
Rotate away from Allium crops for at least four years
Control soil insects such as onion maggots
Avoid root injury during cultivation
prevention: Reduce root wounds and poorly drained conditions
Use long rotation and clean planting material
Protect the basal plate from injury and stress
spread_conditions: Warm soil temperatures around 25C to 30C
Soil moisture fluctuations or poor drainage
Root damage from maggots or tools
organic_treatment: Use Trichoderma viride or Bacillus subtilis in the soil before planting
Use soil solarization during hot months where feasible
yield_impact: This disease can be highly destructive in both field and storage and may wipe out large portions of a crop.
notes: The rot starts at the bottom basal plate, unlike neck rot which starts from the top of the bulb.

severity_levels.low: Slight leaf-tip yellowing with early root browning
severity_levels.medium: Stunted growth, significant root loss, and soft basal plate
severity_levels.high: Plant death in the field or complete bulb rot in storage

summary: A major Alternaria disease of onion that produces purple-brown concentric lesions with yellow halos, causes leaf collapse, and can also injure seed stalks and bulbs.
symptoms: Small water-soaked lesions that develop white centers
Lesions enlarge and turn purplish-brown with concentric rings and yellow halos
Older leaves are very susceptible and may collapse or die
Bulb infection can produce spongy deep yellow to wine-red rot
causes: Fungal infection
The fungus overwinters on crop debris and volunteer Alliums
treatment: Use foliar fungicides such as Tebuconazole, Difenoconazole, or Mancozeb
Add a sticker or surfactant to improve adhesion on waxy onion leaves
precautions: Use a 2 to 3 year crop rotation
Control onion thrips carefully
Ensure good drainage and air circulation
Plow down crop residues deeply
prevention: Reduce thrips injury because damaged tissue becomes highly susceptible
Scout older leaves early in warm humid weather
Protect seed stalks as well as foliage under pressure
spread_conditions: Warm weather around 25C to 30C with humidity around 80% to 90%
Heavy dew or frequent rains
Thrips damage that increases susceptibility
organic_treatment: Use copper oxychloride sprays
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens foliarly
Use Neem oil to help suppress thrips
yield_impact: This disease can reduce yield by about 30% to 50% and may heavily damage seed crops.
notes: Purple blotch is frequently associated with thrips injury, so vector management is part of disease control.

severity_levels.low: A few small spots with yellow halos
severity_levels.medium: Distinct purple blotches and leaf collapse
severity_levels.high: Severe dieback, broken seed stalks, and bulb infection

summary: A foliar rust of onion that produces orange to reddish-brown pustules, yellows the foliage, and reduces photosynthetic area when humidity and leaf wetness remain favorable.
symptoms: Small white or yellowish flecks appear first on leaves
Bright orange to reddish-brown pustules then develop
Pustules rupture the epidermis and release spores
Heavily infected leaves yellow, wither, and die prematurely
causes: Fungal infection
Wind-blown urediniospores
treatment: Use systemic fungicides such as Tebuconazole, Propiconazole, or Azoxystrobin
Begin spraying at the first sign of rust pustules
precautions: Rotate with non-Allium crops
Remove wild Allium weeds near the field
Avoid excessive nitrogen
Orient rows to improve airflow and sunlight exposure
prevention: Reduce alternate hosts and dense humid canopies
Scout older leaves regularly
Treat before pustules spread across younger leaves
spread_conditions: High humidity and prolonged leaf wetness
Cool to moderate temperatures around 10C to 20C
High soil nitrogen
organic_treatment: Use sulfur or copper-based fungicidal dusts or sprays
Apply Bacillus subtilis
yield_impact: Yield loss is often around 10% to 20% because foliage area is reduced.
notes: Other Alliums such as garlic, leeks, and chives can serve as alternate hosts.

severity_levels.low: Scattered orange pustules on older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Pustules spread to younger leaves with visible yellowing
severity_levels.high: Heavy pustule coverage, premature leaf death, and stunted bulbs

summary: A viral disease complex of onion involving viruses such as onion yellow dwarf virus or iris yellow spot virus, causing streaking, distortion, spotting, stunting, and reduced bulb or seed yield.
symptoms: OYDV can cause yellow streaks, flattening, crinkling, and flopping of leaves
IYSV can produce diamond-shaped straw-colored to white lesions on leaves and scapes
Overall growth becomes stunted
Bulb size and quality decline
causes: Viral infection
OYDV is spread by aphids and IYSV by onion thrips
Infected sets or bulbs used for planting
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the viruses themselves
Manage vectors intensively, for example with products used against thrips or aphids where appropriate
precautions: Use virus-free seeds and sets
Remove volunteer onions and weeds that harbor vectors
Avoid planting near overwintering Allium crops
prevention: Control aphids and thrips early
Use clean planting stock
Rogue infected plants when practical
spread_conditions: High aphid or thrips populations
Dry warm weather that favors thrips multiplication
Continuous Allium cultivation
organic_treatment: Use reflective mulches to repel vectors in suitable systems
Apply Neem oil and insecticidal soaps for vector suppression
Rogue infected plants early
yield_impact: Yield loss may range from about 20% to 60%, and seed crops can be especially vulnerable.
notes: Vector management is central because the viral pathogens themselves cannot be cured once infection occurs.

severity_levels.low: Faint yellow streaks or a few diamond lesions with near-normal growth
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable flattening or spotting with slight stunting
severity_levels.high: Severe distortion or lodging and strongly stunted bulbs

summary: A bacterial foliar blight of onion that begins as water-soaked lesions and progresses to elongated tan or brown necrosis, leaf collapse, and major canopy loss under warm wet conditions.
symptoms: Small water-soaked irregular lesions on leaves
Lesions expand into white tan or brown elongated necrotic areas
Advanced lesions weaken leaf tissue and cause collapse
There is no fuzzy growth or visible spore mass on lesions
causes: Bacterial infection
Introduction through infected seed or transplants
Survival in crop debris and weeds
treatment: Chemical control is difficult and largely preventive
Copper bactericides mixed with EBDC fungicides such as Mancozeb can help suppress spread
precautions: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Avoid overhead irrigation
Work fields only when foliage is dry
Plow crop debris under quickly after harvest
prevention: Reduce splash dispersal and mechanical injury
Start with clean planting material
Scout warm wet fields early
spread_conditions: High temperatures above 27C
High humidity and frequent rainfall
Overhead irrigation and mechanical damage
organic_treatment: Use copper hydroxide or copper octanoate sprays
Use strict sanitation and remove infected plants
yield_impact: Yield reduction can range from about 20% to 50% because the leaf canopy is destroyed early.
notes: It may resemble fungal blights, but it lacks concentric rings and fuzzy growth.

severity_levels.low: Scattered water-soaked spots on outer leaves
severity_levels.medium: Lesions merge and leaf tips begin browning
severity_levels.high: Massive leaf collapse and severely reduced bulb growth

summary: A fungal blight of onion that often follows prior injury from mildew, thrips, or hail and produces dark-centered lesions, black spore masses, and strong foliage or seed-stalk blighting.
symptoms: Small light yellow to brown water-soaked spots appear on leaves
Spots elongate into oval lesions with dark brown to black centers
Sooty black spore masses develop on lesions
Lesions merge and entire leaves or seed stalks may blight and die
causes: Fungal infection
It commonly invades tissue already damaged by downy mildew, thrips, or hail
The pathogen survives in infected plant debris
treatment: Use fungicides such as Iprodione, Boscalid, or Difenoconazole
It is often managed alongside purple blotch spray programs
precautions: Control primary diseases and pests that create entry points
Remove or bury crop residues after harvest
Provide good air drainage in field layout
prevention: Reduce thrips injury and prior leaf damage
Scout during warm humid periods when damaged tissue is present
Protect foliage before secondary blighting accelerates
spread_conditions: Warm weather around 18C to 25C
Extended leaf wetness above 16 hours and high humidity
Pre-existing plant damage
organic_treatment: Use copper fungicides
Apply Bacillus subtilis biological sprays
Use Neem-based thrips management to reduce entry wounds
yield_impact: Bulb yield losses can reach about 20% to 40%, and seed yields may be strongly reduced when stalks fail.
notes: It often occurs with purple blotch and may be hard to separate in the field without closer examination.

severity_levels.low: Small yellow or brown spots on older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Lesions enlarge, black centers appear, and dieback progresses
severity_levels.high: Severe blackening and blighting cause major leaf-area loss

summary: The pigeonpea crop appears healthy, with a vigorous green canopy, normal branching and pod set, and no clear evidence of webbing damage, severe spotting, or sterile bushy growth. Healthy pigeonpea performance is supported by good drainage, warm-season establishment, balanced fertility without excess vegetative stimulation, clean field conditions, and early scouting so leaf, mite, and caterpillar problems are caught before they spread.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and normally expanded without strong mottling, heavy spotting, webbing, or premature yellowing
Plants maintain normal branching, flowering, and pod development for the crop stage
The canopy stays reasonably vigorous without major defoliation or the green barren bushy appearance associated with sterility mosaic
Healthy stands usually show good drought tolerance and productive pod bearing when moisture, spacing, and field sanitation are managed well
causes: Use of healthy seed and good stand establishment
Warm-season growth in well-drained soils
Balanced crop management with appropriate spacing and sanitation
Early observation and response to pests, diseases, and mite vectors
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue routine scouting for leaf spots, webbing caterpillars, and sterility mosaic symptoms
Maintain field sanitation and avoid keeping volunteer or ratooned pigeonpea near the crop
Support crop vigor with balanced nutrition and avoid conditions that create dense humid canopy stress
precautions: Use clean seed and establish the crop in well-drained fields
Avoid prolonged waterlogging and unmanaged volunteer hosts
Maintain spacing that supports airflow and scouting access
Remove suspicious sterile or heavily infested plants early if symptoms appear later
prevention: Use crop rotation and field sanitation
Monitor mite and caterpillar pressure early
Maintain healthy stand structure through proper spacing and weed control
Protect the crop from prolonged stress that weakens flowering and pod filling
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is supported by warm conditions, proper drainage, and balanced stand management
Reduced weed and volunteer host pressure helps limit disease and vector buildup
Regular scouting helps preserve canopy and reproductive health
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care, sanitation, and scouting
Use soil-building organic inputs as appropriate for the field condition
Rely on monitoring and timely removal of infected or heavily infested material rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy pigeonpea crop is more likely to maintain normal flowering, pod setting, grain fill, and harvestable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official pigeonpea package-of-practice recommendations and the absence of the major model-target disease and pest symptoms.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease or major pest symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative and reproductive growth

summary: A fungal leaf spot disease of pigeonpea that begins on older leaves as brown lesions and can progress to yellowing, defoliation, and reduced pod filling under warm humid conditions.
symptoms: Small circular to irregular brown or dark brown spots on older leaves
Spots may develop a grayish or pale center as they mature
Under severe infection, spots merge and leaves yellow and drop prematurely
Dark fungal growth may appear on the lower leaf surface in high humidity
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives in infected crop debris from previous seasons
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides such as Mancozeb at 2.5 g/L or Carbendazim at 1 g/L
Spray at first appearance of symptoms and repeat after about 15 days if weather remains favorable
precautions: Use deep summer ploughing to destroy infected debris
Maintain wider row spacing to improve airflow
Rotate with non-leguminous crops
prevention: Reduce carryover residue and dense humid canopy conditions
Scout older leaves first because disease often begins there
Protect the crop before severe defoliation starts
spread_conditions: High relative humidity above 80 percent
Warm temperatures around 25C to 30C
Continuous rainfall and dense crop canopy
organic_treatment: Spray Neem Seed Kernel Extract at 5 percent
Use foliar applications of Pseudomonas fluorescens
yield_impact: Yield loss is often around 10% to 20%, but can rise if severe defoliation occurs before pod formation.
notes: It is often a minor disease but can become much more important in unusually wet seasons.

severity_levels.low: A few scattered brown spots on lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots enlarge and merge with noticeable yellowing of the lower canopy
severity_levels.high: Severe spotting causes massive premature defoliation and poor pod filling

summary: A webbing caterpillar problem of pigeonpea in which larvae tie leaves, shoots, flowers, or pods together and feed from inside the silken mass, causing papery patches, drying, and yield loss.
symptoms: Larvae fold and web leaves, terminal shoots, and flower buds together
They feed on chlorophyll inside the webbed mass and leave white papery patches
Webbed terminal shoots dry and turn brown
In severe cases flowers and pods are also webbed and eaten, causing shedding
causes: Infestation by larval webbing moths
Eggs are laid on tender leaves and flower buds
treatment: Apply insecticides such as Indoxacarb, Spinosad, Emamectin benzoate, or Quinalphos
Direct spray into the canopy so it penetrates the webs
precautions: Use intercrops such as sorghum or maize that encourage natural enemies
Avoid overly close spacing that increases canopy overlap
Use light or pheromone traps to monitor moth activity
prevention: Scout early because older larvae are protected inside webs
Avoid excessive nitrogen that encourages dense tender canopy
Reduce repeated monocropping where pest pressure persists
spread_conditions: Cloudy warm weather with intermittent rainfall
Dense crop canopy and excessive nitrogen use
Monocropping without rotation
organic_treatment: Use Bacillus thuringiensis formulations
Spray Neem oil or NSKE 5 percent at early larval stages
Remove and destroy webbed leaves in small plantings
yield_impact: Yield loss can range around 15% to 30%, especially when attack extends into flowering and podding stages.
notes: Sprays often fail if applied too late because the larvae are protected by strong silken webs.

severity_levels.low: A few webbed leaves with minimal impact
severity_levels.medium: Multiple webbed shoots with some delay in flowering
severity_levels.high: Extensive webbing of flowers and pods with heavy bud drop and yield reduction

summary: A severe viral disease of pigeonpea that causes mosaic mottling, bushy green sterile growth, and major yield loss because infected plants produce few or no pods.
symptoms: Leaves show clear light and dark green mosaic mottling
Leaves become smaller, narrower, and clustered, giving a bushy appearance
Plants are stunted with increased vegetative branching
Flower and pod production stop partially or completely
Infected plants stay green late into the season while healthy plants mature and dry
causes: Viral infection caused by pigeonpea sterility mosaic virus
Transmission by the eriophyid mite Aceria cajani
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the virus itself
Chemical control focuses on the mite vector using acaricides or wettable sulfur early in the infection cycle
precautions: Grow resistant or highly tolerant varieties where available
Rogue infected plants as soon as they are noticed, especially early in the season
Avoid ratooning or keeping perennial pigeonpea near the field
prevention: Reduce mite sources and volunteer pigeonpea hosts
Act early before infection spreads across the stand
Do not keep green infected plants as carryover reservoirs
spread_conditions: High populations of the mite vector
Presence of volunteer pigeonpea plants or perennial wild hosts
Mild temperatures and high humidity favor mite multiplication and wind dispersal
organic_treatment: Use strict field sanitation and roguing
Apply repeated Neem oil sprays to deter mite feeding and breeding
yield_impact: Infection at the seedling stage can cause complete crop failure, and severe early infection may lead to nearly 100% loss.
notes: This disease is widely known as the green plague of pigeonpea; green bushy non-flowering plants among mature drying plants are a classic field sign.

severity_levels.low: Mild mosaic on upper leaves with partial flowering and little stunting
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable bushy growth with major reduction in pods
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting and completely barren plants without pods

summary: The ragi crop appears healthy, with an even green stand, normal tillering and finger development, and no clear evidence of green-ear distortion, viral mottling, seedling collapse, smut sori, or basal wilt. Healthy ragi performance is supported by well-drained fertile soil, balanced nutrient application, timely irrigation without water stagnation, clean seed, and regular field scouting through seedling, tillering, and earhead stages.
symptoms: Leaves are evenly green and normally expanded without strong chlorotic striping, mosaic mottling, severe basal rot, or black smut sacs on fingers
Plants maintain normal tillering, canopy vigor, and earhead development for the crop stage
The field stand remains reasonably uniform without major seedling gaps, collapsed patches, or rapidly wilting plants
Healthy earheads develop and mature normally without conversion into leafy green-ear structures or widespread grain loss
causes: Use of healthy seed and proper crop establishment
Well-drained relatively fertile soils with balanced fertility
Timely irrigation and avoidance of prolonged water stagnation
Good field sanitation and early observation of disease or pest symptoms
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue scouting the crop for seedling mortality, chlorotic streaking, green-ear symptoms, and basal foot rot
Maintain water management that supports growth but avoids prolonged waterlogging
Support crop vigor with balanced nutrient application and timely interculture
precautions: Avoid water stagnation because poorly drained fields favor several serious ragi diseases
Use clean seed and a well-prepared seedbed or nursery
Maintain proper spacing and field hygiene
Remove suspicious diseased plants early if clear symptoms develop later
prevention: Use healthy seed and crop rotation where needed
Keep the field well drained and avoid prolonged stress
Monitor crop health from seedling stage through earhead formation
Maintain balanced fertility and avoid careless carryover of infected debris
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is favored by well-drained soils, balanced nutrition, and proper irrigation
Reduced standing water and good field hygiene help limit early disease development
Routine scouting preserves plant stand and reproductive health
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care, drainage management, and scouting
Use organic manures or biological soil-support practices as appropriate for the field
Rely on observation and sanitation rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy ragi crop is more likely to maintain normal tillering, finger formation, grain fill, and harvestable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official ragi crop-production recommendations and the absence of the major model-target disease symptoms.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth and earhead development

summary: A destructive downy mildew or green-ear disease of ragi that causes chlorotic striping, stiff thickened leaves, excessive tillering, and conversion of earheads into leafy structures with major grain loss.
symptoms: Plants become stunted with thickened broadened stiff leaves
Leaves show pale green or chlorotic stripes
Lateral branches and tillers proliferate, creating a bushy appearance
Earheads may transform into leafy twisted green structures known as green ear or crazy top
Affected plants fail to produce normal grains
causes: Oomycete pathogen infection
The pathogen survives as oospores in soil and infected crop debris
Infected seed may also carry the pathogen
treatment: Use seed treatment with Metalaxyl before sowing
Spray Metalaxyl plus Mancozeb at early vegetative stage if symptoms appear
precautions: Ensure proper drainage and avoid water stagnation
Use certified disease-free seed
Use deep summer ploughing to reduce oospore survival
Rotate with non-cereal crops
prevention: Protect seed and seedlings from prolonged saturated soil
Rogue infected plants quickly before inoculum builds up
Reduce carryover debris and poorly drained nursery or field spots
spread_conditions: Waterlogged or poorly drained soils
High soil moisture and flooding during seedling stage
Cool to moderate temperatures with high humidity
organic_treatment: Use seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens or Trichoderma viride
Improve drainage with raised beds or proper land shaping
Rogue and burn infected plants immediately
yield_impact: This disease can cause roughly 10% to 40% yield loss in poorly drained fields because affected earheads become barren.
notes: The green-ear symptom is the most distinctive field sign because the grain head converts into a leafy mass.

severity_levels.low: Mild chlorotic stripes with slight stunting
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable leaf thickening and some deformed earheads
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting and complete green-ear transformation with total grain loss on affected plants

summary: A viral mottle disease of ragi that causes chlorotic streaking or mosaic patterns, stunting, and poor earhead formation when infection occurs early.
symptoms: Light green to yellow mottling and streaking parallel to the leaf veins
Leaves become pale and may show mosaic patterns
Plants can become severely stunted when infected early
Earheads may be small, deformed, and poorly filled or barren
causes: Viral infection
Transmission by insect vectors such as leafhoppers or aphids depending on the strain
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the virus itself
Focus management on early vector control with systemic insecticides where recommended
precautions: Grow resistant or tolerant ragi varieties where available
Maintain a weed-free field and remove grassy borders
Use early planting to avoid peak vector populations
Rogue infected plants early
prevention: Suppress vectors before heavy spread occurs
Remove alternate weed hosts around the field
Do not confuse persistent streaking and mottling with simple nutrient deficiency
scientific_name: Eleusine mosaic virus (EMV) / Ragi mottle streak virus
spread_conditions: High populations of vector insects
Presence of wild grassy hosts
Warm dry weather that favors vector multiplication
organic_treatment: Use Neem Seed Kernel Extract or Neem oil to repel vectors
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor vectors
Promote natural predators such as ladybird beetles
yield_impact: Early infection can cause about 50% to 80% yield loss because plant growth and ear formation are strongly reduced.
notes: The vein-parallel streaking and mosaic pattern help distinguish viral mottle from simple nutrient stress.

severity_levels.low: Faint mottling on younger leaves with little stunting
severity_levels.medium: Distinct streaking and mosaic with moderate stunting and reduced finger size
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, strongly chlorotic leaves, and barren or highly deformed earheads

summary: A seedling blight or damping-off problem of ragi in which seeds rot before emergence or seedlings collapse after emergence because of soil- or seed-borne fungi.
symptoms: Seeds may rot before emergence in the soil
Seedlings may collapse at the soil line after emergence
Brown to black water-soaked lesions appear on coleoptile and roots
Leaves of affected seedlings yellow, wilt, and dry
White mycelium may be visible at the base in some infections
causes: Seed-borne or soil-borne fungal pathogens
Infection during germination and the early seedling stage
treatment: Use seed treatment fungicides such as Thiram, Captan, or Carbendazim
Use soil drenching with Copper oxychloride or Carbendazim if nursery patches begin to blight
precautions: Use certified healthy seed
Avoid sowing too deep and prepare a well-drained seedbed
Avoid excess nitrogen in the nursery stage
Use crop rotation to reduce soil inoculum
prevention: Protect seed before sowing because prevention is much more effective than rescue
Avoid persistently wet nursery conditions
Improve nursery hygiene and drainage
spread_conditions: Excessive soil moisture or poor drainage
Deep sowing of seed
Use of untreated infected seed
Cool wet soils for some pathogens and warm wet soils for others
organic_treatment: Use seed treatment with Trichoderma viride or Pseudomonas fluorescens
Mix well-decomposed farmyard manure with Trichoderma in nursery beds
Use nursery-bed solarization before sowing where feasible

notes: Early control is critical because stand loss at the seedling stage is hard to recover later.

severity_levels.low: Scattered seedling death and slight stand thinning
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable patches of dead seedlings and poor establishment
severity_levels.high: Massive seedling collapse requiring major resowing

summary: A floral smut of ragi in which individual grains are converted into black spore-filled sacs, lowering grain quality and causing localized yield loss within the earheads.

The sacs rupture and release dusty black spores
Usually only scattered grains within an earhead are affected
Affected grains may swell and project out of the glumes

Teliospores survive in soil and crop debris
treatment: Use seed treatment with Carboxin or Carbendazim
Use foliar sprays such as Propiconazole, Mancozeb, or Carbendazim at booting or early flowering if pressure is high
precautions: Use clean healthy seed
Rotate crops to reduce spore loads
Remove infected earheads carefully before sori rupture
prevention: Protect the crop around flowering when weather is favorable for infection
Reduce inoculum carryover from previous seasons
Rogue infected heads before they disperse spores
spread_conditions: High humidity and frequent light rains during flowering
Moderate temperatures
Carryover inoculum from previous seasons
organic_treatment: Use seed treatment with Trichoderma viride
Use hot-water seed treatment where appropriate
Rogue infected earheads into bags before spores spread
yield_impact: Yield loss is usually around 5% to 10%, but produce quality and marketability are reduced.

severity_levels.low: One or two grains per ear affected
severity_levels.medium: Several grains per ear become smutted with visible spore dust
severity_levels.high: A high share of grains is destroyed across the field and quality drops sharply

summary: A soil-borne foot rot or wilt of ragi that attacks the basal stem and roots, causing yellowing, basal decay, white mycelial growth, sclerotia formation, and plant death in patches.

Brown water-soaked rot develops at the stem base near the soil line

Use spot application to help protect nearby healthy plants
precautions: Use deep summer ploughing to expose and destroy sclerotia
Avoid planting in heavily infested or waterlogged fields
Rotate with non-host crops where possible
Use only well-decomposed organic manures

Remove wilted plants with nearby soil to take away sclerotia

Warm temperatures around 25C to 35C
Accumulation of undecomposed organic matter near the surface
Mechanical damage at the plant base

Apply neem cake before sowing
Remove and destroy wilted plants with surrounding soil

severity_levels.low: A few isolated plants show wilting and basal rot
severity_levels.medium: Patches of plants wilt and gaps appear in the canopy
severity_levels.high: Rapid spread causes extensive plant death and large barren patches

summary: The rice crop appears healthy, with a uniform green canopy, normal tillering, steady leaf expansion, and no clear evidence of bacterial blight streaking, brown spot lesions, or black leaf-smut sori. Balanced fertility with sensible nitrogen management
Appropriate water management with drainage when needed
Regular field sanitation, weed control, and monitoring

Maintain field sanitation and reduce weed or stubble carryover
Use water management and spacing practices that reduce prolonged leaf stress

Maintain balanced nutrition and proper water control
Remove or bury infected residues after harvest
Scout seedlings and main-field rice regularly through tillering and reproductive stages

Reduced carryover stubble and timely weeding help maintain stand health
Routine scouting helps catch disease before it becomes canopy-wide

Use organic amendments and biological support practices according to local production needs
Rely on field monitoring and timely water management rather than unnecessary sprays

notes: Rice has no explicit healthy class in the current model bundle, but this advisory record is stored so healthy-crop guidance is available for future healthy-routing logic.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal seedling or canopy development

summary: A major bacterial disease of rice that begins as water-soaked streaks from the leaf tips or margins and can progress to extensive blighting and seedling collapse under wet stormy conditions.
symptoms: Water-soaked yellowish-white streaks start from leaf tips and margins
Lesions enlarge and become greyish-white with wavy edges
Entire leaves may dry up and die in severe cases
Milky opaque bacterial ooze may appear on young lesions early in the morning
Seedlings may collapse in the kresek phase
causes: Bacterial infection entering through wounds or hydathodes
The pathogen survives in infected stubble, seed, and alternate hosts
treatment: There is no highly effective rescue treatment once severe outbreak is established
Preventive copper-based bactericides may help suppress spread
Streptomycin and tetracycline combinations are sometimes used where legally permitted
precautions: Grow resistant varieties where available
Apply nitrogen in split doses rather than in heavy excess
Ensure drainage and avoid prolonged flooded nursery stress
Destroy weed hosts and incorporate infected stubble after harvest
prevention: Reduce wind- and rain-driven injury where possible
Avoid excessive nitrogen that encourages severe disease
Scout both nursery and main field during wet windy periods
spread_conditions: Heavy rainfall, high humidity, and strong winds

Waterlogged conditions and flooded nurseries

Apply Neem Seed Kernel Extract or Neem oil
Use seed treatment and foliar application of Pseudomonas fluorescens

Use hot-water seed treatment where appropriate

Remove or plough under infected residues after harvest

Manage the crop to reduce prolonged leaf wetness in dense stands

Dense canopy that keeps leaves wet
Airborne spores and splashing rain help spread

Manage nutrients with compost and avoid synthetic nitrogen spikes

severity_levels.medium: Numerous black spots on upper leaves with slight tip yellowing

summary: The sorghum crop appears healthy, with a uniform stand, good leaf color, normal stalk strength, and properly developing heads without obvious anthracnose lesions, rust pustules, smut sori, or grain mold. Balanced soil fertility and regionally adapted crop management
Rotation and field sanitation that reduce disease carryover
Regular observation of the crop before stress becomes canopy-wide

Harvest promptly once maturity is reached to protect grain quality

Avoid carrying infected residue and volunteer sorghum between seasons
Use varieties suited to the field and season
Do not ignore head health late in the season because grain quality can decline quickly
prevention: Use clean seed and crop rotation
Maintain balanced fertility and avoid unnecessary crop stress
Scout foliage and heads routinely from vegetative stage through grain fill
Keep fields and borders clean to reduce alternate hosts and volunteer carryover

Rotation and weed management reduce background pest and disease pressure
Routine scouting protects both vegetative growth and head quality

Use soil-building organic inputs and rotation as appropriate for the production system
Rely on good agronomic management rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy sorghum crop is more likely to maintain stalk strength, grain set, grain quality, and harvestable yield.
notes: Sorghum has no explicit healthy class in the current model bundle, so this advisory record is stored to keep healthy-crop guidance available for future routing logic.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal vegetative growth, head development, and stand health

severity_levels.medium: Lesions merge upward and slight red discoloration appears in stalks
severity_levels.high: Severe leaf blighting, stalk lodging, and poor head development

summary: A grain mold complex of sorghum in which multiple fungi colonize developing or mature grains, causing mold growth, shriveling, discoloration, poor viability, and major grain-quality loss.
symptoms: Developing or mature grains are covered with white, pinkish, or dark fungal growth

Airborne spores land on susceptible panicles

Control head bugs and similar grain-feeding insects that create entry wounds

Use looser or more open panicle types when possible
Harvest promptly at physiological maturity
prevention: Reduce moisture retention around heads and protect grains from insect injury
Dry grain quickly after harvest
Scout heads closely during flowering and maturation in wet conditions

Compact panicles that trap moisture
Insect damage that creates grain wounds
organic_treatment: Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens at flowering
Harvest on time and dry grains quickly and thoroughly

severity_levels.low: Light mold on a few grains per panicle
severity_levels.medium: Visible mold covers parts of the panicle and some grains shrivel
severity_levels.high: Large portions of the panicle mold heavily and grain quality collapses

Avoid contaminating healthy seed during threshing or storage
Keep infected harvest lots separate

Bag and destroy infected heads before the membrane ruptures

Remove infected heads before spores spread across the field
Use resistant genetics and rotation together

Continuous sorghum cropping increases risk

Use crop rotation as the main organic defense

severity_levels.low: A few isolated infected plants
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable patches of smutted heads and moderate spore release

Spray at the first sign of pustules on lower leaves

Avoid late sowing when possible
Remove alternate hosts and volunteer sorghum around the field

Watch late-planted crops especially closely
Reduce volunteer carryover around fields

Cool to moderate temperatures around 16C to 25C

severity_levels.high: Leaves dry and die early, reducing grain size and fodder quality

Occasionally also soil-borne
Wind-blown spores may infect open flowers and contribute to secondary spread

Grow resistant varieties where available
Collect and destroy infected heads before spores disperse

Keep harvest and seed lots free from contamination

summary: The soybean crop appears healthy, with a uniform green canopy, normal nodulation and vegetative growth, good flowering or pod set for the stage, and no clear evidence of blight lesions, mosaic distortion, powdery growth, rust pustules, stem-base collapse, or sudden-death leaf scorch. Healthy soybean performance is supported by clean seed, proper Rhizobium nodulation, balanced fertility and soil pH management, adequate moisture without prolonged saturation, good weed control, and regular scouting throughout vegetative and reproductive stages.

Plants maintain normal branching, flowering, and pod development for the crop stage

Timely weed, insect, and disease scouting

Maintain balanced fertility and irrigation or moisture management according to field need

Use crop rotation, soil-building practices, and sanitation as the foundation of management
Rely on timely scouting and agronomic management rather than unnecessary sprays

notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official soybean production references and the absence of the major model-target disease symptoms.

summary: A bacterial foliar disease of soyabean that begins as angular water-soaked leaf spots and may progress to yellowing, blackening, and tattered foliage under cool wet weather.

Dark lesions can also form on stems and pods

Avoid field operations when the canopy is wet

Field work when foliage is wet

severity_levels.medium: Noticeable tearing and yellowing spreading toward mid-canopy

The pathogen survives in infected soybean residue on the soil surface

Bury or decompose residue where appropriate
Improve field drainage

Scout from the bottom of the canopy upward
Protect the crop before defoliation reaches the upper canopy

Rain splash moves spores from residue to lower leaves
Continuous soybean cropping

Apply Bacillus subtilis
Promote full residue incorporation where suitable

severity_levels.low: Spotting remains in the lower third of the canopy
severity_levels.medium: Yellowing and spotting reach mid-canopy with minor leaf drop
severity_levels.high: Premature defoliation reaches the upper canopy and pods are stunted

Seed coats may show dark mottling

Transmission mainly by aphids
The virus is also seed-borne

Aphid control may reduce some secondary spread but often does not prevent initial transmission
precautions: Plant certified virus-free seed
Use resistant soybean varieties
Avoid late planting that coincides with peak aphid movement

Do not carry infected seed lots into future planting
Monitor for seed mottling where food-grade quality matters

Cool temperatures where symptoms express more clearly
Late planting

Use reflective mulches or border crops to slow aphid entry

The pathogen survives on living host tissue or alternate hosts

Avoid late planting
Maintain spacing that improves air movement

Treat before heavy canopy coverage occurs
Do not assume low humidity means no fungal risk because this pathogen does not need leaf wetness to infect

Low humidity relative to many other foliar fungi
Late planting or late-maturing varieties

Apply Neem oil
Use baking soda or potassium bicarbonate solutions

summary: Asian soybean rust is a highly destructive foliar disease of soyabean that develops first as tiny leaf spots and pustules, then drives rapid yellowing, browning, and defoliation if fungicides are delayed.

Spray at the first sign of disease or when regional alerts indicate imminent risk

Use earlier-maturing varieties where escape is possible
Use wider row spacing to reduce canopy humidity

Do not wait for severe symptoms because losses escalate quickly
Scout the lower canopy closely under humid conditions

Moderate temperatures around 15C to 28C
Dense canopy closure that traps humidity

Seed treatments may provide only limited early protection

Maintain strong weed control

Scout patch edges and crown tissue early
Remove obvious inoculum sources where practical

High soil moisture followed by drying
Sandy or well-aerated soils with high undecomposed organic matter

Compost added organic matter thoroughly before use

Protect roots and manage SCN

Compacted soils or poor drainage
