prevention: Begin whitefly management early, especially within the first month of crop establishment
Reduce nearby host reservoirs
Use clean seed and early monitoring of vector pressure
scientific_name: Mungbean Yellow Mosaic India Virus (MYMIV) / Begomovirus
spread_conditions: High whitefly populations
Warm dry weather favoring whitefly breeding and movement
Presence of alternate legume hosts
organic_treatment: Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and catch whiteflies
Apply Neem oil to disrupt whitefly feeding and breeding
Promote natural predators such as lacewings
yield_impact: Yield loss can range from about 30% to total crop failure when infection begins early.
notes: This is a major tropical and subtropical threat, and vector control must start very early in crop development.

severity_levels.low: A few yellow specks appear on younger leaves
severity_levels.medium: Distinct yellow mosaic with visible stunting
severity_levels.high: The canopy turns bright yellow and pod set largely fails

summary: A Septoria leaf spot record aligned to the separate `septoria` classifier label in this model bundle. It represents the same disease entity as brown spot in soyabean and is stored separately so RAG retrieval matches either classifier output exactly.
symptoms: Small irregular dark brown spots form first on lower older leaves
Surrounding tissue turns yellow
Premature leaf drop can move upward from the bottom of the plant
Late in the season stems and pods may show brown irregular lesions
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives in infected soybean residue on the soil surface
treatment: Use foliar fungicides such as Azoxystrobin, Pyraclostrobin, or Fluxapyroxad between about R3 and R5 when disease pressure and economics justify spraying
precautions: Rotate at least one year with a non-host crop such as corn
Bury or decompose residue where appropriate
Improve field drainage
prevention: Reduce residue carryover and prolonged lower-canopy wetness
Scout from the bottom of the canopy upward
Protect the crop before defoliation reaches the upper canopy
spread_conditions: Warm wet weather from about 15C to 30C with frequent rainfall
Rain splash moves spores from residue to lower leaves
Continuous soybean cropping
organic_treatment: Use copper-based fungicides
Apply Bacillus subtilis
Promote residue incorporation where suitable
yield_impact: Yield loss is often about 5% to 15%, but can exceed 20% if defoliation reaches the upper canopy during seed fill.
notes: This model treats brown spot and septoria as separate labels, even though they usually refer to the same disease concept in soyabean.

severity_levels.low: Spotting remains in the lower third of the canopy
severity_levels.medium: Yellowing and spotting reach mid-canopy with minor leaf drop
severity_levels.high: Premature defoliation reaches the upper canopy and pods are stunted

summary: The sugarcane crop appears healthy, with vigorous green leaves, normal stalk elongation, good tillering for the stage, and no clear evidence of chlorotic banding, rust pustules, grassy-shoot proliferation, spindle distortion, sett failure, or black smut whips. Healthy sugarcane performance is supported by clean planting material, balanced nutrition, timely irrigation and drainage, clean cultivation, and careful monitoring of both seed-sett health and ratoon carryover.
symptoms: Leaves are green and functional without major banding, pustules, mosaic mottling, or strong drying beyond normal lower-leaf aging
Stalks develop normally with good girth, internode growth, and canopy vigor for the crop stage
Plants do not show grassy clump formation, spindle twisting, severe top injury, or extensive sett-gap symptoms
Healthy fields usually maintain strong stand continuity when setts are sound, irrigation is timely, and ratoon hygiene is good
causes: Use of healthy certified seed setts and good field establishment
Balanced fertilization and soil moisture management
Good drainage, clean cultivation, and ratoon hygiene
Early detection and removal of diseased stools or setts
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue regular scouting for sett failure, spindle symptoms, rust, yellowing, grassy shoots, and smut whips
Maintain irrigation and drainage according to field condition
Support cane vigor with balanced fertility and timely weed control
precautions: Use pest- and disease-free setts for planting
Avoid carrying diseased ratoon material forward
Maintain clean cultivation and drainage channels
Harvest mature crop on time and avoid unnecessary stress
prevention: Use healthy planting material and resistant varieties where available
Manage ratoons carefully and avoid keeping heavily infected fields
Maintain balanced nutrition and irrigation
Scout the crop regularly from establishment through maturity
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is supported by clean planting material, good water management, and balanced nutrition
Reduced ratoon carryover and clean cultivation lower disease pressure
Routine scouting helps protect both cane growth and seed-sett quality
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care, drainage management, and sanitation
Use healthy sett selection, organic soil-building practices, and careful ratoon management as core tools
Rely on observation and timely removal of diseased stools rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy sugarcane crop is more likely to maintain cane tonnage, juice quality, and ratoon productivity.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official sugarcane production recommendations and the absence of the major model-target disease and stress symptoms.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the crop shows normal canopy, stalk, and stand development

summary: A physiological stress symptom of sugarcane in which leaves develop horizontal pale bands after sudden environmental or chemical stress, while tissue above and below the band often remains green.
symptoms: Distinct horizontal pale yellow to white bands run across the leaf blade
Bands usually appear on leaves that were in the spindle stage when stress occurred
There is no rotting in early stages
Leaf tissue above and below the band often remains normally green
causes: Extreme temperature fluctuations such as very cold nights or frost
Temporary heat stress or scorching
Herbicide drift causing localized bleaching
treatment: No chemical treatment is usually needed because plants often recover as weather normalizes
Maintain adequate soil moisture and nutrition so the cane can outgrow the stress
precautions: Adjust planting dates to avoid extreme temperature exposure during sensitive growth
Use irrigation to moderate severe heat or frost stress where feasible
Avoid herbicide drift
prevention: Reduce exposure to avoidable climatic or spray stress
Keep plants vigorous so recovery is faster
Differentiate this symptom from infectious leaf streaking before reacting
spread_conditions: Sudden climatic drops in temperature
Late winter or early spring plantings exposed to frost
organic_treatment: Use seaweed extracts or biostimulants to help reduce abiotic stress
yield_impact: Yield effect is usually negligible unless the growing point is damaged.
notes: This is not an infectious disease and the horizontal banding pattern helps separate it from viral streaking symptoms.

severity_levels.low: Faint banding on a few leaves with no growth impact
severity_levels.medium: Distinct white bands with temporary growth slowing
severity_levels.high: Severe bleaching causes tissue death or breakage at the band

summary: A fungal leaf spot of sugarcane that causes reddish-brown lesions with yellow halos on foliage and may reduce photosynthetic area when older leaves become heavily affected.
symptoms: Small oval reddish-brown spots appear on both leaf surfaces
Spots often have a prominent yellow halo
Older lesions develop greyish or tan centers
Severe infection causes lesions to merge and leaves to yellow and dry early
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives on infected crop debris and older leaves
treatment: Apply foliar fungicides such as Mancozeb or Carbendazim when symptoms appear early and disease pressure justifies treatment
Cultural practices are often the main control
precautions: Use resistant varieties where available
Remove dry or infected leaves through detrashing
Maintain spacing and airflow
prevention: Reduce lower-leaf inoculum and dense humid canopy conditions
Scout older leaves before disease moves upward
Use clean field hygiene and variety choice
spread_conditions: High humidity and frequent rainfall
Warm temperatures around 25C to 30C
Dense canopy and poor airflow
organic_treatment: Use detrashing to reduce inoculum
Apply Pseudomonas fluorescens
yield_impact: Yield loss is often around 5% to 15% in susceptible varieties because photosynthetic area is lost.
notes: Usually a relatively minor disease but it can still reduce cane growth and sugar recovery if drying starts early.

severity_levels.low: A few isolated spots on lower older leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots spread into the middle canopy and older leaves yellow
severity_levels.high: Severe spotting causes early foliage drying and reduced cane girth

summary: A serious rust disease of sugarcane that forms reddish-brown pustules on leaves, leading to leaf death, shorter internodes, and reduced cane yield in susceptible varieties.
symptoms: Yellowish spots form first and then turn reddish-brown
Spots develop into raised pustules that release reddish-brown spores
Heavy infection kills leaf tissue from the tip downward
Severe disease causes stunting and shorter internodes
causes: Fungal infection
Wind-blown urediniospores from infected fields
treatment: Apply fungicides such as Propiconazole, Tebuconazole, or Azoxystrobin early in disease development
precautions: Grow rust-resistant varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Harvest mature cane early if rust is severe
prevention: Reduce susceptibility through variety choice and balanced nutrition
Scout before pustules occupy the upper canopy
Respond early because rust can spread fast under favorable weather
spread_conditions: High humidity, frequent dew, or light rainfall
Moderate temperatures around 15C to 25C
Excessive nitrogen increases susceptibility
organic_treatment: Use wettable sulfur sprays
Maintain balanced nutrition including potassium and silicon where relevant
yield_impact: Yield loss can range around 15% to 40% in susceptible varieties, with lower juice quality as well.
notes: Spores rub off easily onto fingers or clothing, leaving a rusty brown smudge.

severity_levels.low: Scattered pustules occur on lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Pustules spread upward and the field takes on a reddish-brown cast
severity_levels.high: Widespread leaf death and strong reduction in cane tonnage

summary: A stress-related sugarcane disorder in which leaves dry prematurely because of drought, nutrient imbalance, salinity, waterlogging, or severe physiological decline rather than a single infectious pathogen.
symptoms: Lower leaves yellow, brown, and dry first
Leaves become brittle and papery
Growth slows and cane girth shrinks
Severe cases may produce hollow internodes or pithiness
causes: Severe soil-moisture deficit
Nutrient deficiencies such as low nitrogen or potassium
Salinity or waterlogging that damages roots
Natural senescence of lower leaves in mild cases
treatment: Restore irrigation or moisture availability promptly
Use foliar nutrient support such as potassium nitrate or urea where nutrient stress is confirmed
precautions: Use scheduled irrigation based on soil moisture or field observation
Apply trash mulching to conserve soil moisture
Use soil testing to guide fertilization
prevention: Distinguish natural lower-leaf drying from whole-plant stress
Reduce drought and salinity stress
Maintain balanced fertilization and root-zone health
spread_conditions: Prolonged dry spells without irrigation
Poorly structured sandy or compacted soils
High temperatures and low humidity
organic_treatment: Use compost or farmyard manure to improve water-holding capacity
Use trash mulching
yield_impact: Severe drying during grand growth can reduce cane tonnage and juice recovery by roughly 20% to 60%.
notes: Always separate normal bottom-leaf senescence from true whole-plant stress before diagnosing a problem.

severity_levels.low: Only the lowest leaves dry, often as a normal aging response
severity_levels.medium: Drying advances upward and growth slows
severity_levels.high: The canopy dries extensively, stalks become pithy, and juice content drops sharply

summary: A devastating phytoplasma disease of sugarcane that causes profuse thin pale shoots, severe stunting, and failure to produce millable canes, especially in ratoon crops.
symptoms: Numerous thin weak grassy tillers emerge from the base
Leaves are narrow short and often pale yellow or white
Plants are stunted and fail to form millable cane
Lateral buds sprout prematurely
causes: Phytoplasma infection
Use of infected seed canes
Transmission by insect vectors such as aphids and leafhoppers
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the phytoplasma itself
Manage vectors early with systemic insecticides where appropriate
precautions: Use certified healthy seed cane
Do not ratoon infected fields
Uproot and destroy infected clumps immediately
prevention: Keep infected stools and seed sources out of the planting chain
Reduce vector pressure and ratoon carryover
Use heat-treated or otherwise sanitized seed material
spread_conditions: Continuous ratooning of infected crops
High vector populations
Planting uncertified infected seed material
organic_treatment: Use moist hot air treatment of seed setts before planting
Practice strict field roguing
yield_impact: Affected clumps are usually a total loss, and heavily infected ratoon crops can lose most of their productive cane.
notes: The white thin grassy shoots are highly distinctive and the disease is especially destructive in ratoon crops.

severity_levels.low: A few tillers in a stool show mild yellowing
severity_levels.medium: Entire stools become grassy and fail to form millable cane
severity_levels.high: Widespread infection causes major ratoon crop failure

summary: A fungal disease of sugarcane spindle and young top growth that causes chlorosis, twisting, wrinkling, knife-cut lesions, and in severe cases top rot and death of the growing point.
symptoms: Chlorosis appears at the base of young spindle leaves
Leaves become distorted, wrinkled, twisted, and shortened
Reddish-brown lesions may appear on leaves and stalks, sometimes giving knife-cut symptoms
Severe cases develop top rot and kill the growing point
causes: Fungal infection
Airborne spores infect spindle leaves
treatment: Apply fungicides such as Carbendazim, Copper Oxychloride, or Mancozeb directed at the spindle when early chlorotic symptoms are seen
precautions: Use resistant or tolerant varieties
Avoid excessive nitrogen that causes lush susceptible growth
Control top borer insects that create entry points
prevention: Watch spindle leaves closely during humid rapid-growth periods
Reduce insect injury that favors infection
Respond early before top rot develops
spread_conditions: High temperature and humidity, often at monsoon onset
Rapid growth phase of cane
Insect injury such as top borer damage
organic_treatment: Use Pseudomonas fluorescens sprays
Apply Neem seed kernel extract to deter boring insects
yield_impact: Yield loss is often about 5% to 10%, but top rot cases may cause much larger losses through severe stunting.
notes: The name means distorted top, and many plants can partially recover when conditions turn drier.

severity_levels.low: Mild chlorosis and slight wrinkling of spindle leaves
severity_levels.medium: Leaf distortion and knife-cut lesions become obvious
severity_levels.high: Top rot develops and the growing point dies

summary: A sett-borne and soil-borne rot of planted sugarcane cuttings that causes poor germination, blackened internal tissue, field gaps, and a characteristic pineapple-like odor.
symptoms: Planted setts fail to germinate and gaps appear in the field
Internal sett tissue turns red, then brown to black with hollowing
Infected setts smell like overripe pineapple
Black soot-like fungal growth may develop inside the rotting sett
causes: Soil-borne and sett-borne fungal pathogen
The fungus enters through cut ends of setts
treatment: Dip cut setts in fungicide solutions such as Carbendazim or Thiophanate Methyl before planting
precautions: Select healthy 8 to 10 month old seed canes
Make clean cuts with sharp knives
Avoid deep planting and ensure a well-drained seedbed
prevention: Protect setts before planting because later rescue is limited
Avoid waterlogging, compaction, and delayed germination
Use healthy seed-cane sources consistently
spread_conditions: Poorly drained or compacted soils
Deep planting
Dry cold or overly wet conditions that delay germination
organic_treatment: Use sett treatment with Trichoderma viride or Pseudomonas fluorescens
Smear cut ends with cow dung slurry where locally practiced
yield_impact: This disease reduces stand establishment and can cut initial plant population by roughly 20% to 60%.
notes: The ripe-pineapple smell from a failed sett is a classic field diagnosis clue.

severity_levels.low: Germination is delayed and rotting is limited to the ends
severity_levels.medium: Noticeable rotting causes 10% to 20% bud-sprout failure
severity_levels.high: Setts rot completely and large field gaps require replanting

summary: A mosaic-type viral disease of sugarcane that causes light and dark green mottling on leaves, stunting, and thinner canes, especially when infected seed setts are repeatedly used.
symptoms: Leaves show contrasting light and dark green mottling or mosaic
Symptoms are most visible on the basal part of young rapidly growing leaves
Susceptible varieties may show reddening or necrosis
Plants become stunted and canes remain thinner
causes: Viral infection
Transmission by aphid vectors
Spread through infected seed setts
treatment: There is no chemical cure for the virus
Manage aphid vectors with systemic insecticides where appropriate
precautions: Use certified virus-free seed setts
Plant resistant or tolerant varieties
Keep fields and surrounding areas free of grassy weed hosts
Rogue infected plants early
prevention: Break the cycle by using clean seed material
Reduce aphid and alternate-host pressure
Do not carry infected seed cane into ratoon or new planting
spread_conditions: High aphid populations
Presence of alternate grass weed hosts
Continuous use of uncertified seed cane
organic_treatment: Use meristem-tip-derived disease-free seed nurseries
Apply Neem oil to deter aphids
yield_impact: Yield loss often ranges around 10% to 30%, and losses can compound in subsequent ratoon crops.
notes: Holding leaves toward sunlight can make mosaic symptoms easier to see.

severity_levels.low: Faint mottling occurs on young leaves with little stunting
severity_levels.medium: Distinct mosaic and moderate stunting are visible
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, yellowing or necrosis, and major tonnage loss occur

summary: A viral or phytoplasma-associated yellow leaf disease of sugarcane that begins with yellowing of the midrib on the underside of leaves and may progress to bunchy top appearance, reduced cane thickness, and lower sucrose recovery.
symptoms: The lower surface midrib becomes intensely yellow, often on leaves three to six from the top
Yellowing spreads from the midrib into the leaf blade
Leaves dry from tip toward base
Shortened internodes near the top produce a bunchy top appearance
Canes become thinner and overall growth is stunted
causes: Infection by sugarcane yellow leaf virus or phytoplasma
Transmission by sugarcane aphids
Spread through infected setts
treatment: There is no chemical cure
Targeted aphid control may help slow field spread
precautions: Use resistant varieties where available
Plant virus-free seed cane from tissue-culture or clean sources
Do not ratoon highly infected fields
Reduce drought stress that intensifies symptom expression
prevention: Use clean seed and manage aphid pressure early
Avoid carrying infection into ratoon crops
Monitor lower-surface midribs on mid-aged leaves as crop matures
spread_conditions: Warm weather that increases aphid populations
Ratooning of infected crops
Stress such as drought that makes symptoms more obvious
organic_treatment: Use strict tissue-cultured or meristem-derived seed setts
Use Neem oil for aphid management
yield_impact: Yield losses often range about 20% to 50% and sucrose content can also fall sharply.
notes: The bright yellow midrib on the underside of leaves is a hallmark field sign.

severity_levels.low: Yellowing remains mostly on the midrib with little growth effect
severity_levels.medium: Yellowing spreads into the blade and bunchy top becomes visible
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, canopy drying, and major yield and sugar losses occur

summary: A major smut disease of sugarcane that produces the characteristic black whip from the growing point, stunts the cane, and causes severe economic loss in susceptible crops and ratoons.
symptoms: A long black whip-like structure emerges from the central growing point
The whip is first covered by a thin silvery membrane which soon ruptures
After rupture it releases millions of black powdery chlamydospores
Affected canes are stunted, thin, and produce excessive lateral tillers
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen is sett-borne and soil-borne, and airborne spores also infect standing crops
treatment: Use sett treatment by soaking in Carbendazim or Triadimefon before planting
Foliar fungicides are generally ineffective once the whip forms
precautions: Plant highly resistant varieties
Use certified disease-free setts
Avoid ratooning fields with meaningful smut incidence
Bag the whip before removal to avoid spore dispersal
prevention: Use clean planting material and heat treatment where appropriate
Rogue infected clumps completely, not just the whip
Do not allow ratoon carryover from infected crop
spread_conditions: Hot dry weather favors spore dispersal and infection
Ratooning infected fields increases incidence strongly
Plant injuries can aid infection
organic_treatment: Use moist hot air treatment of setts
Rogue infected stools strictly
yield_impact: Yield loss can reach roughly 30% to 70%, and infected stalks are effectively unmillable.
notes: Never pull a whip openly in the field because it can release huge numbers of spores onto neighboring plants.

severity_levels.low: Only isolated whips are seen with minimal spread
severity_levels.medium: Multiple stools produce whips and moderate stunting is visible
severity_levels.high: Whips are widespread, thin useless canes proliferate, and economic loss becomes severe

summary: The sunflower crop appears healthy, with broad green leaves, normal turgor, a strong hairy stem, and no clear evidence of downy mildew, gray mold, or non-healing mechanical injury. Healthy sunflower performance is supported by good stand establishment, clean seed, balanced fertility, proper spacing, well-managed water, and ongoing scouting so stress and disease are detected before heads or leaves are heavily affected.
symptoms: Leaves show a uniform deep green color without lesions, mold, strong chlorosis, or major tearing
Leaves remain broad, flat, and appropriately turgid rather than distorted or wilted
Stems are strong and green with normal hairs and no obvious rotting or collapse
Plants show normal vigorous growth for their stage, with healthy head and canopy development
causes: Optimal environmental conditions
Balanced soil nutrition and adequate moisture
Effective pest and disease management
treatment: No treatment is required at this stage
Continue scouting for leaf symptoms, head rot, and stress injuries as the crop develops
Maintain water and fertility so the crop stays vigorous without excess stress
precautions: Maintain proactive scouting for pests and diseases
Follow recommended agronomic practices for spacing, irrigation, and field hygiene
Use balanced fertilizers based on soil test and field need
prevention: Use clean seed and keep the field well managed from emergence onward
Avoid prolonged stress from poor drainage or drought
Maintain strong canopy health and low weed pressure
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is supported by balanced fertility, good establishment, and appropriate water management
Good spacing and field monitoring help maintain healthy leaves and heads
Low disease and insect pressure preserve normal growth
organic_treatment: Maintain soil health with compost and beneficial microbes to support natural resilience
Rely on preventive crop care and monitoring rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy sunflower plant is more likely to reach its full seed and oil yield potential.
notes: This is the healthy baseline class in the model and should be treated as a normal, non-problem state rather than a disease.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the plant shows normal healthy growth

summary: A systemic downy mildew disease of sunflower that infects through the roots, severely stunts plants, produces white growth under leaves, and often prevents normal head development.
symptoms: Plants become severely stunted, often far shorter than normal
Leaves thicken, feel leathery, and margins curl downward
Chlorosis often appears bordering leaf veins on the upper surface
Dense white cottony growth appears on the underside of leaves
Heads may point upright instead of bending naturally
causes: Soil-borne oomycete pathogen
Infection occurs through roots from oospores surviving in soil
The pathogen can also be seed-borne
treatment: Systemic seed treatments such as Metalaxyl, Mefenoxam, Oxathiapiprolin, or Azoxystrobin are the most effective defense
Foliar fungicides are usually ineffective once systemic stunting has developed
precautions: Plant genetically resistant hybrids
Use a crop rotation of three to four years away from sunflower
Delay planting until soils are warm and well drained
Remove volunteer sunflower and wild Helianthus hosts
prevention: Avoid poorly drained fields and early planting into cool wet soils
Use clean seed and resistant genetics
Reduce long-term soil inoculum through rotation
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 12C to 18C with heavy rainfall or waterlogging soon after planting
Poorly drained fields
Repeated cultivation of susceptible sunflower
organic_treatment: Use strict long-term rotation
Improve soil structure and drainage
Use certified clean organic seed
yield_impact: Systemically infected plants often produce little to no viable seed, so yield loss on those plants is near total.
notes: A patch of severely stunted upright plants with white growth beneath leaves is highly characteristic.

severity_levels.low: Rare late-season localized leaf spotting with minimal impact
severity_levels.medium: A few systemically infected plants appear in wet or poorly drained spots
severity_levels.high: Widespread stunting causes severe yield loss or total field failure in bad outbreaks

summary: A destructive head-rot disease of sunflower that begins as brown water-soaked lesions on the back of the head and progresses to soft rot with characteristic grey dusty mold under humid conditions.
symptoms: Brown water-soaked lesions form on the back of the sunflower head
Lesions become soft and may spread across the entire head
A grey dusty fuzzy mold develops on infected tissue in humid weather
Black resting bodies may form on decaying tissue
Seeds become empty, shriveled, or drop early
causes: Fungal infection by Botrytis cinerea
The fungus survives on dead plant material and many alternate hosts
treatment: Use fungicides such as Boscalid, Pyraclostrobin, or Fludioxonil around early flowering
Control is often difficult because the downward-facing head limits spray coverage
precautions: Avoid low-lying or poorly ventilated fields
Reduce plant density to improve canopy airflow
Control birds and insects that wound heads
Harvest as early as possible after physiological maturity
prevention: Keep heads dry where possible and reduce injury that opens infection sites
Scout the back of heads closely during cool wet weather
Do not delay harvest when head rot is active
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 15C to 20C with prolonged humidity, dew, or rain
Wet weather during flowering and seed ripening
Mechanical or insect or bird wounds on the head
organic_treatment: Use biological fungicides such as Bacillus subtilis or Trichoderma species
Use strict weed management to improve airflow
yield_impact: Losses can range from modest damage to complete destruction of harvestable seed when heads rot severely.
notes: Botrytis mold is grey and dusty, which helps separate it from some other head rots.

severity_levels.low: Small brown spots and minor grey mold are visible on parts of the head
severity_levels.medium: Rot spreads across the receptacle and part of the seed is lost
severity_levels.high: The head rots heavily, may drop from the stalk, and major yield loss occurs

summary: A non-infectious class covering healed mechanical or physiological damage on sunflower leaves and stems, including natural leaf-drop scars and dry tears from wind, hail, or past insect feeding.
symptoms: Callused corky crescent-shaped marks may appear on stems where older leaves dropped
Leaves may show irregular dry brown or white papery holes and torn edges
Damaged areas have clean dry margins without fuzzy mold, ooze, or spreading halos
Most surrounding tissue remains green and otherwise healthy
causes: Natural senescence of older leaves
Mechanical damage from wind, sandblasting, or hail
Healed wounds from earlier chewing insect injury
treatment: No chemical treatment is needed because scars represent already healed or non-pathogenic damage
If fresh storm injury occurs, some growers use protective measures only to reduce opportunistic infection risk
precautions: Use wind protection where feasible
Monitor and control chewing insects before severe defoliation occurs
Maintain adequate plant nutrition so the crop can recover quickly
prevention: Reduce physical stress and insect chewing pressure early
Recognize healed scars correctly so unnecessary sprays are avoided
Support plant vigor to help it outgrow damage
spread_conditions: This condition does not spread because it is non-infectious
Occurrence often tracks past severe weather or insect chewing events
organic_treatment: Use biostimulants or seaweed extracts to help plants recover from physical stress
yield_impact: Yield effect is usually minimal unless severe weather removed a large share of photosynthetic area during a critical stage.
notes: This class is important because it prevents non-pathogenic damage from being mistaken for a disease that needs spraying.

severity_levels.low: A few wind tears or natural stem scars are visible
severity_levels.medium: Moderate physical damage is noticeable but the growing point remains intact
severity_levels.high: Severe tearing removes much of the leaf area and may delay maturity

summary: The tomato crop appears healthy, with well-colored foliage, normal stem growth, good flower or fruit set for the stage, and no clear evidence of blights, bacterial spotting, mold, mite bronzing, or viral distortion. Healthy tomato performance is supported by disease-free transplants, well-drained fertile soil, drip irrigation, balanced fertility, good staking or trellising, and regular scouting so lower-leaf and vector-driven problems are caught early.
symptoms: Leaves are green and functional without major spotting, mold growth, bronzing, strong curling, or severe yellowing
Plants maintain normal branching, flowering, and fruit development for their stage
Stems stay sturdy without severe lesions or collapse
Fruit develops without obvious greasy blight lesions, scabby bacterial spots, or virus-related ripening problems
causes: Use of healthy disease-free transplants or seed
Balanced soil fertility and suitable pH
Consistent moisture through drip or careful irrigation
Good staking, airflow, and routine crop monitoring
treatment: No disease treatment is indicated at this stage
Continue regular scouting for lower-leaf spots, whitefly or mite pressure, and new viral symptoms
Maintain even soil moisture without overhead wetting of foliage
Support crop vigor with balanced fertilization and canopy management
precautions: Use disease-free transplants and clean seed
Use drip irrigation instead of overhead watering where possible
Maintain good airflow with spacing, staking, and moderate pruning
Avoid carrying diseased debris or working plants when wet
prevention: Rotate away from solanaceous crops when possible
Keep foliage off soil with staking or cages
Monitor vectors like whiteflies and mites early
Clean up old crop debris promptly
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is supported by well-drained soil, balanced nutrition, and consistent moisture
Good airflow and reduced foliage wetness help prevent many tomato diseases
Regular scouting limits the chance of unnoticed lower-canopy disease spread
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; continue preventive crop care, sanitation, and monitoring
Use mulch, drip irrigation, and biological support products where they fit the production system
Rely on good crop management rather than unnecessary sprays
yield_impact: A healthy tomato crop is more likely to maintain fruit set, fruit quality, flavor, and harvestable yield.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official tomato production recommendations and the absence of the major model-target disease and pest symptoms.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease or major pest symptoms are detected and the plant shows normal vegetative and reproductive growth

summary: A bacterial disease of tomato that causes dark water-soaked leaf spots, yellowing, leaf drop, and scabby fruit lesions that make fruit unmarketable.
symptoms: Small water-soaked dark irregular spots appear on older leaves
Leaf spots may show a yellow halo and eventually lead to leaf yellowing and drop
Fruit develops dark raised scab-like crusty lesions with a water-soaked margin
Lesions do not show concentric rings
causes: Bacterial infection
Contaminated seed or infected transplants
The pathogen survives in volunteer tomato plants and crop debris
treatment: Use preventive copper-based bactericides combined with Mancozeb
Control becomes difficult once disease is widespread, so prevention is critical
precautions: Use certified disease-free seed and transplants
Use drip rather than overhead irrigation
Rotate two to three years away from solanaceous crops
Avoid working plants when wet
prevention: Reduce splash dispersal and infected planting material
Use sanitation and rotation to limit carryover
Act before fruit infection occurs because fruit scabs cannot be reversed
spread_conditions: Warm weather around 24C to 30C
High humidity and heavy rainfall
Overhead irrigation and splashing spread bacteria
organic_treatment: Use copper octanoate or copper soap sprays
Practice strict sanitation and debris removal
Apply Bacillus subtilis foliar sprays
yield_impact: Losses can range about 20% to 50%, mainly because fruit becomes scabbed and foliage loss causes sunscald.
notes: Prevention and sanitation are critical because infected fruit cannot be cured.

severity_levels.low: A few dark spots appear on lower leaves
severity_levels.medium: Foliage spotting and some defoliation with minor fruit injury
severity_levels.high: Severe defoliation exposes fruit to sunscald and heavy scabbing makes fruit unmarketable

summary: A very common fungal disease of tomato that starts on lower leaves and causes dark bullseye lesions, yellowing, stem injury, and fruit rot near the calyx.
symptoms: Brown to black spots appear first on older lower leaves
Spots develop distinct concentric bullseye rings
Surrounding tissue often turns strongly yellow
Stem lesions are dark and sunken
Fruit develops dark leathery sunken spots with concentric rings near the stem end
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives in soil and infected plant debris for long periods
treatment: Use protectant or systemic fungicides such as Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Azoxystrobin
Begin treatment at the first sign of lower-leaf spotting
precautions: Use spacing and pruning to improve airflow
Stake or cage plants to keep foliage off the soil
Mulch around the base to reduce soil splash
prevention: Protect lower leaves first because disease usually starts there
Reduce plant stress from poor nutrition or heavy fruit load
Keep debris and infected leaves from building up near plants
spread_conditions: Warm temperatures around 24C to 29C
High humidity, heavy dew, or frequent rain
Plant stress such as poor nutrition or heavy fruit load
organic_treatment: Use copper-based fungicides
Apply Neem oil and Bacillus subtilis
Remove infected lower leaves consistently
yield_impact: Yield losses often range from about 20% to 50%, mainly through foliage loss and fruit sunscald.
notes: The bullseye ring pattern is the most important field clue.

severity_levels.low: Bullseye spots remain on the lowest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots move upward with moderate yellowing and lower leaf drop
severity_levels.high: Severe defoliation and fruit rot near the stem become common

summary: A highly destructive oomycete disease of tomato that causes water-soaked leaf lesions, white growth under humid conditions, dark stem lesions, and rapid plant collapse.
symptoms: Large irregular water-soaked pale green to brown spots form on leaves
White fuzzy growth appears on the underside of lesions in high humidity
Dark brown to black stem lesions may girdle tissue
Fruit develops large firm dark greasy-looking spots
causes: Oomycete pathogen
Spores can travel long distances on wind currents
The pathogen survives on living tissue rather than dead debris
treatment: Use fungicides such as Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or targeted oomycete products preventively
Once established it is very difficult to stop
precautions: Use resistant varieties where possible
Destroy volunteer potato plants and nearby nightshade weeds
Monitor local late blight alerts
prevention: Act preventively because outbreaks move extremely fast
Remove infected plants quickly to protect nearby crop
Do not rely on waiting for heavy symptom development
spread_conditions: Cool temperatures around 10C to 20C
Wet rainy weather and prolonged high humidity
The disease spreads extremely rapidly under these conditions
organic_treatment: Use preventive copper sprays
Remove and destroy infected plants immediately
yield_impact: This disease can destroy an entire crop within days under favorable conditions.
notes: It is the same pathogen associated historically with the Irish potato famine and it can melt down tomato crops very quickly.

severity_levels.low: A few water-soaked leaf spots appear, though disease rarely stays mild for long
severity_levels.medium: Leaf and stem lesions expand rapidly and greasy fruit spots appear
severity_levels.high: The plant collapses and rots within a few days

summary: A greenhouse- and tunnel-favored fungal disease of tomato that causes yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces and olive-green velvety mold on the undersides of leaves.
symptoms: Pale green to yellowish spots appear on the upper surface of older leaves
The underside of those spots develops olive-green to brown velvety fungal growth
Infected leaves yellow, wither, and drop
Fruit is rarely infected directly, but defoliation reduces fruit size
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives on crop debris or greenhouse structures
treatment: Use fungicides such as Difenoconazole, Chlorothalonil, or Mancozeb
Begin spraying when the first yellow spots appear
precautions: Ensure strong ventilation and air circulation in protected structures
Reduce humidity by venting and heating if needed
Prune lower leaves to improve air movement
Avoid wetting foliage
prevention: Manage humidity aggressively in greenhouses and tunnels
Remove infected foliage early
Do not allow dense lower-canopy humidity to persist
spread_conditions: Relative humidity above 85 percent
Warm temperatures around 22C to 26C
Poor air circulation
organic_treatment: Use copper fungicides
Improve environmental conditions because ventilation is the most important organic control
yield_impact: Yield loss is usually indirect and often around 10% to 30% because foliage is lost.
notes: This disease is especially associated with high tunnels and greenhouses rather than open well-ventilated fields.

severity_levels.low: Faint yellow spots and minor mold appear on the oldest leaves
severity_levels.medium: Lower and middle canopy develop widespread yellowing and velvety mold
severity_levels.high: Premature defoliation becomes severe and fruit growth is reduced

summary: A fungal leaf disease of tomato that produces many small spots with pale centers and black pycnidia, causing heavy defoliation but usually not direct fruit infection.
symptoms: Numerous small circular spots appear on lower leaves
Spots have dark brown margins and tan to light grey centers
Tiny black pycnidia can be seen in lesion centers
Heavily infected leaves yellow, dry, and drop
The fruit is not infected directly
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives in tomato debris and on solanaceous weeds
treatment: Use fungicides such as Chlorothalonil, Mancozeb, or Copper preventively or at first spotting
precautions: Mulch heavily to reduce soil splash
Use drip or base watering
Stake plants and keep lower foliage off the ground
Clean up all crop debris after the season
prevention: Protect lower leaves first because disease commonly begins there
Remove the first infected leaves promptly to slow upward spread
Avoid frequent splash dispersal from the soil surface
spread_conditions: Moderate temperatures around 20C to 25C
High humidity and rainfall
Splashing water from soil to lower leaves
organic_treatment: Use copper sprays
Remove the first infected leaves promptly
yield_impact: Yield losses often range from about 20% to 40%, mainly because defoliation reduces fruit quality and increases sunscald risk.
notes: Septoria spots are smaller and more numerous than early blight spots, and they have black dots rather than bullseye rings.

severity_levels.low: Tiny scattered spots occur on the bottommost leaves
severity_levels.medium: Spots move into the mid-canopy and lower leaf yellowing and drop increase
severity_levels.high: The plant becomes heavily defoliated and fruit is exposed to severe sunscald

summary: A hot-weather tomato pest problem in which mites cause fine stippling, leaf bronzing, webbing, and potentially severe foliage loss if outbreaks are not suppressed early.
symptoms: Tiny yellow or white stippling appears on upper leaf surfaces
Leaves become pale, bronze, or fully yellow and dry
Fine silken webbing appears under leaves and at stem tips
Tiny moving mites may be seen under leaves with magnification
causes: Sap-sucking arachnids feeding on plant cells
Mites are dispersed by wind or moved on clothing or tools
treatment: Use specific miticides such as Abamectin, Spiromesifen, or Bifenazate
Coverage must reach the undersides of leaves
precautions: Keep plants well watered during hot dry weather
Wash foliage occasionally with a strong jet of water to reduce dust and mites
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill predatory mites
prevention: Manage outbreaks early before webbing and bronzing become severe
Reduce dusty conditions and plant stress
Preserve natural enemies where possible
spread_conditions: Hot dry dusty weather
Water-stressed plants
Loss of natural predators after broad-spectrum insecticide use
organic_treatment: Use Neem oil or horticultural oils
Apply insecticidal soaps
Release predatory mites such as Phytoseiulus persimilis
yield_impact: Losses can range roughly from 30% to 80% when severe outbreaks develop early in hot weather.
notes: Because mites are arachnids rather than insects, many ordinary insecticides do not work and can worsen outbreaks.

severity_levels.low: Minor stippling occurs on a few leaves and webbing is absent
severity_levels.medium: Leaves bronze or yellow and webbing becomes visible
severity_levels.high: Foliage dries heavily, webbing covers the plant, and death may follow

summary: A fungal disease of tomato that can resemble early blight but often affects upper foliage more readily and causes fruit lesions with lighter centers.
symptoms: Small brown leaf spots with yellow halos enlarge into lesions with concentric rings
Unlike early blight, young and old leaves may both be affected
Stem lesions are dark brown and elongated
Fruit develops sunken dark circular lesions often with a light brown center
causes: Fungal infection
The pathogen survives in crop debris and has a wide host range
treatment: Use fungicides such as Chlorothalonil or suitable systemic products where resistance issues allow
Early blight spray programs often suppress target spot as well
precautions: Use pruning and spacing to improve airflow
Avoid overhead watering
Rotate crops and clear debris quickly
prevention: Do not assume all ringed spots are early blight
Manage canopy humidity and protect foliage before upper-canopy spread accelerates
Keep fruit protected by maintaining canopy health
spread_conditions: High humidity and heavy rainfall
Warm temperatures around 20C to 28C
Dense canopies that hold moisture
organic_treatment: Use copper-based fungicides
Practice aggressive pruning and canopy management
yield_impact: Yield losses often range from about 15% to 40% through combined foliage and fruit damage.
notes: Fruit lesions often have lighter centers and upper foliage may be affected faster than in typical early blight.

severity_levels.low: Scattered spots resemble minor early blight
severity_levels.medium: Moderate defoliation and some fruit spotting occur
severity_levels.high: Severe defoliation and widespread fruit rot occur

summary: A highly stable tobamovirus disease of tomato that causes mottled leaves, growth distortion, stunting, and uneven fruit ripening, and spreads easily through handling and contaminated tools.
symptoms: Leaves show a clear light and dark green mosaic pattern
Leaves may be stunted, curled, or become fern-like
The plant loses vigor and becomes stunted
Fruit may ripen unevenly, develop yellow blotches, or show internal browning
causes: Viral infection in the tobamovirus group
The virus spreads mechanically by hands, tools, clothing, and infected seed
Contamination may also be linked to tobacco products or infected debris
treatment: There is no chemical cure
Remove and destroy infected plants rather than composting them
precautions: Use resistant varieties
Wash hands thoroughly before handling plants
Disinfect pruning tools frequently
Use certified virus-free seed
prevention: Maintain strict hygiene and tool sanitation
Do not move plant sap between plants during pruning or tying
Start with clean seed and remove infected plants promptly
spread_conditions: Handling plants during pruning, tying, or harvest
The virus survives for long periods on tools, clothing, and debris
organic_treatment: Use strict sanitation and hygiene
Use seed sanitation treatments where appropriate if seed is not pre-treated
yield_impact: Yield loss can range around 10% to 50%, and fruit quality can drop sharply.
notes: This virus is extremely stable and can survive on dry tools or debris for long periods.

severity_levels.low: Mild leaf mottling and slight vigor reduction occur
severity_levels.medium: Leaf distortion, delayed fruit set, and uneven ripening appear
severity_levels.high: Severe stunting, fern-like foliage, and strong fruit-quality loss occur

summary: A highly destructive whitefly-transmitted viral disease of tomato that causes strong upward leaf curling, yellow leaf margins, severe stunting, and heavy flower loss.
symptoms: Leaves curl strongly upward and become cup-shaped
Leaf margins turn yellow while veins remain greener
Leaves appear crumpled, crinkled, and reduced in size
Plants become severely stunted if infected early
Flowers abort and fruit set drops drastically
causes: Viral infection by a begomovirus
Transmission exclusively by sweetpotato whitefly
treatment: There is no cure for the virus
Use early whitefly control with systemic insecticides where appropriate to limit spread
precautions: Use resistant or tolerant varieties such as Ty lines
Use insect netting in protected structures
Remove weeds around the crop
Destroy infected plants immediately
prevention: Repel and suppress whiteflies early before plants reach flowering
Reduce alternate hosts nearby
Do not carry infected plants within or between plantings
spread_conditions: High whitefly populations
Hot dry weather that favors whitefly breeding and movement
Presence of alternate weed hosts
organic_treatment: Use reflective silver mulch to repel whiteflies
Use yellow sticky traps to monitor vectors
Apply Neem oil or insecticidal soaps to leaf undersides
yield_impact: Early infection can cause about 50% to total crop failure because flowering and fruit set collapse.
notes: A miniature yellow-edged crinkled plant with heavy flower drop is a classic visual clue for TYLCV.

severity_levels.low: Newest growth shows mild curling and some fruit still sets
severity_levels.medium: Distinct yellowing, cupping, stunting, and flower drop occur
severity_levels.high: Severe bonsai-like stunting develops and fruit production nearly stops

summary: The wheat crop appears healthy, with an even green canopy, upright vigorous tillers, intact leaves without blotches or bleaching, and normal growth for the stage. Healthy wheat performance is supported by well-drained soil, timely sowing, balanced fertility, clean seed, appropriate irrigation, and regular scouting so foliar and head diseases are caught before they affect flag leaves or grain filling.
symptoms: Leaves show a uniform green color without blotches, rusting, mildew, blackening, or premature bleaching
Plants maintain normal tillering, stem strength, and canopy structure for the growth stage
Heads and stems develop normally without white heads, shriveled grain symptoms, or obvious disease lesions
The field stand is even, with no strong stunting, collapse, or major lodging linked to disease
causes: Good crop establishment in suitable, well-drained soil
Balanced nutrient and moisture management
Use of clean seed and routine field monitoring
treatment: No disease treatment is needed at this stage
Continue regular scouting through tillering, booting, heading, and grain fill
Maintain irrigation and fertility according to crop stage and field need
Use preventive protection only when local disease risk and forecasts justify it
precautions: Use clean or certified seed and maintain a well-prepared seedbed
Avoid waterlogging and maintain timely irrigation
Apply balanced fertilizer based on soil need rather than excess nitrogen
Keep watching for seedborne, foliar, and head diseases as the crop advances
prevention: Support strong stand establishment with timely sowing and good soil moisture
Maintain crop vigor with balanced nutrition and sound weed management
Reduce disease pressure through sanitation, scouting, and locally appropriate seed protection
spread_conditions: Healthy crop growth is favored by well-drained soil, timely sowing, and suitable moisture
Balanced fertility and careful nitrogen management help maintain a competitive, resilient stand
Routine scouting and clean seed reduce the chance of hidden early disease problems
organic_treatment: No curative treatment is needed; maintain soil organic matter, preventive crop care, and close monitoring
Use compost, green manures, or other approved nutrient sources where they fit the system
yield_impact: A healthy wheat crop is better positioned to realize its yield and grain-quality potential.
notes: Healthy-record guidance is synthesized from official wheat production recommendations emphasizing drainage, balanced fertility, clean seed, timely planting, and ongoing scouting.

severity_levels.none: No clear visible disease symptoms are detected and the wheat canopy shows normal healthy growth

summary: A grain-quality disease complex in wheat that causes black discoloration at the embryo end of kernels, usually becoming obvious near harvest or in cleaned grain lots rather than as a dramatic field symptom.
symptoms: Dark brown to black discoloration appears at the embryo end of the kernel
Blackening may extend along the grain crease
Affected kernels may be slightly shriveled or remain fairly plump depending on timing
Symptoms are usually noticed during harvest, grading, or seed inspection rather than on foliage
causes: Fungal infection of developing grain
A complex of fungi such as Alternaria alternata and Bipolaris sorokiniana often colonizes the grain surface
treatment: Use heading-stage fungicides such as Tebuconazole or Azoxystrobin when wet weather is expected during grain filling
Treat seed intended for the next season with appropriate seed-treatment fungicides such as Thiram or Carboxin
precautions: Harvest promptly once the crop matures
Avoid excessive late irrigation during grain filling
Reduce lodging risk through balanced spacing and nitrogen management
Store grain in cool dry conditions after harvest
prevention: Shorten the period that mature grain remains exposed to wet weather
Dry grain quickly after harvest and keep moisture low in storage
Avoid replanting heavily discolored seed lots
spread_conditions: High humidity, heavy dew, or frequent rainfall during grain fill to dough stage
Delayed harvest in wet weather
Lodging that raises humidity around the heads
organic_treatment: Harvest on time and reduce grain moisture below about 12% quickly
Clean and sort grain to remove heavily affected kernels before storage or planting
yield_impact: Direct yield loss is usually low, but market discounts and poor seed viability can create substantial economic loss.
notes: Black point is mainly a grain-quality and seed-quality problem rather than a major foliage disease.

severity_levels.low: Darkening is confined to the embryo tip and remains mostly cosmetic
severity_levels.medium: Distinct blackening of the germ end causes visible quality downgrade
severity_levels.high: Discoloration spreads broadly over kernels and seed quality drops sharply

summary: A soil- and stubble-borne crown disease of wheat that browns the stem base, weakens tillers, and can produce white heads when drought stress prevents infected stems from supplying water during grain fill.
symptoms: Honey-brown to dark brown discoloration develops at the crown and lower stem base
Plants may be stunted and produce fewer tillers
Prematurely bleached white heads appear on affected tillers or plants
Pinkish or whitish fungal growth may appear beneath lower leaf sheaths near the soil line in humid conditions
causes: Soil-borne and stubble-borne Fusarium infection
The pathogen survives in infected cereal residue and crown tissue
treatment: Use fungicide seed treatments such as Fludioxonil, Tebuconazole, or Difenoconazole for early suppression
Do not rely on foliar fungicides because infection occurs at the crown and lower stem
precautions: Rotate with non-host broadleaf crops for at least one to two years
Manage infected cereal stubble through removal, deep incorporation, or faster breakdown where appropriate
Support plant resilience with sound nutrition including adequate zinc where needed
Avoid sowing too early into warm dry soils
prevention: Break cereal-on-cereal cycles that maintain infected residue
Reduce moisture stress after flowering where irrigation is available
Protect seedlings and crowns early because late white-head expression cannot be reversed
spread_conditions: Infection begins in moist soil and residue, while severe white-head symptoms are intensified by drought during grain fill
Continuous wheat or barley cropping
Surface retention of infected cereal stubble
organic_treatment: Use strict multi-year rotation with non-host broadleaf crops
Consider seed bio-priming with Trichoderma harzianum or similar biological protectants where appropriate
yield_impact: Yield loss can range from moderate to severe, especially when post-flowering moisture stress turns crown infections into widespread white heads.
notes: White heads from crown rot result from stem-base infection disrupting water flow, not from direct head infection as in head blight.

severity_levels.low: Mild basal browning is present without meaningful white-head expression
severity_levels.medium: Basal discoloration is extensive and scattered white heads appear
severity_levels.high: White heads become widespread and grain shriveling causes major yield loss

summary: A warm-weather foliar blight of wheat, often called spot blotch, that starts on lower leaves as dark spots and can move upward until the flag leaf is blighted, sharply reducing grain filling.
symptoms: Small dark brown spots first appear on lower leaves
Spots expand into elongated or irregular dark brown blotches
Lesions often develop a yellow halo
Lesions merge and cause leaves to yellow, dry, and die prematurely
Disease usually advances upward through the canopy toward the flag leaf
causes: Fungal infection
Spores survive on infected seed, soil, and crop residue
treatment: Use foliar fungicides such as Propiconazole, Tebuconazole, or Azoxystrobin plus Cyproconazole
Time sprays between booting and heading when needed to protect the flag leaf
precautions: Use resistant or tolerant varieties where available
Sow on time to avoid late grain fill under warm humid conditions
Rotate with non-cereal crops
Avoid overly dense canopies driven by excess nitrogen
prevention: Prioritize protection of the flag leaf and upper canopy
Reduce inoculum through residue and seed management
Do not let lower-canopy infections build unchecked before heading
spread_conditions: Warm weather around 20C to 30C with high humidity
Frequent rainfall or long leaf wetness periods
Late-sown wheat facing warmer conditions during grain fill
organic_treatment: Use foliar applications of Pseudomonas fluorescens where they fit the program
Rely on rotation and stubble management to reduce inoculum pressure
yield_impact: Losses become serious when the disease reaches the flag leaf because grain filling is then strongly reduced.
notes: In warm humid wheat areas, protecting the flag leaf is the most important practical goal.

severity_levels.low: Lower leaves show scattered spots while the flag leaf remains clean
severity_levels.medium: Blotches reach the middle canopy and lower leaves yellow noticeably
severity_levels.high: Blight reaches the flag leaf and premature drying causes shriveled grain

summary: A highly aggressive spike disease of wheat that suddenly bleaches heads above the infection point and can destroy grain set within days under warm wet heading conditions.
symptoms: Spikes bleach suddenly and prematurely, often starting above the infection point
The stem or lower plant may remain green while the head turns white
Dark grey to black fungal spores may appear on the rachis infection point
Leaves may show eye-shaped lesions with grey centers and dark margins
Grains in bleached sections become shriveled, deformed, or absent
causes: Infection by Magnaporthe oryzae pathotype Triticum
The pathogen is airborne and can also be carried on seed
treatment: Use preventive fungicide mixtures such as Triazoles plus Strobilurins at heading when risk is high
Do not expect curative control once spike symptoms are visible
precautions: Never use seed sourced from infected areas
Adjust sowing dates so heading avoids forecast periods of warm continuous rain
Use tolerant or resistant material where available
Remove alternate hosts and enforce quarantine measures where required
prevention: Prevent introduction through clean seed and movement controls
Protect the crop before heading during high-risk weather windows
Act quickly because epidemic spread can accelerate in only a few days
spread_conditions: Heading or flowering coincides with warm weather around 25C to 30C and continuous rain
Cloudy humid conditions above roughly 85% relative humidity
Long-distance wind dispersal of spores
organic_treatment: There is no reliable organic curative option
Rely on quarantine, clean seed, and rapid destruction of heavily infected crop material where regulations allow
yield_impact: Under epidemic conditions the disease can destroy a large share of the crop very quickly and may cause near-total loss in affected heads or fields.
notes: Wheat blast can resemble Fusarium head blight, but it typically lacks pink mold and may show grey to black sporulation on the rachis.

severity_levels.low: Only a few spikelets or scattered heads show early bleaching
severity_levels.medium: Bleached heads appear in visible patches across the field
severity_levels.high: Large areas whiten rapidly and infected heads produce little or no grain
